**Goal:** Remove money as a source of stress and prove financial reliability.

**Current Level:** Survival — delivering pizzas, living shift to shift.

**Active Quests:**
- [ ] Build emergency buffer: target $500 (even $20/shift set aside)
- [ ] Track every dollar for 2 weeks — know where it goes
- [ ] Research Stray Kids tour dates and ticket prices — start a dedicated savings stash for this
- [ ] Identify one additional income stream (even small: selling items, side gig, tips optimization)
- [ ] When access to house returns: take over a bill. Even one. Proves financial partnership.

**Leveling Metric:** You have enough buffer that a blown tire doesn't end your week. That's level 1.

**ADHD Hack:** Cash envelope system. Physical separation of money into categories. Pizza money goes into envelopes: rent, tire fund, Stray Kids fund, food, flex. Visual and tactile — works better for ADHD than spreadsheets.

---

### 🧠 MIND (Mental Health & Self-Knowledge)
**Goal:** Understand your own hardware so you can operate it instead of it operating you.

**Active Quests:**
- [ ] **THIS WEEK: Find VA psychiatry number and save it in phone**
- [ ] Schedule psychiatrist appointment — full evaluation, not just ADHD
- [ ] Be honest about: pattern flooding, bliss states, history of marijuana use, mold exposure
- [ ] Continue catechumen classes
- [ ] Jesus prayer daily — minimum 5 minutes morning, 5 minutes before bed
- [ ] When spiraling: voice dump → 3 minutes max → put phone down

**Leveling Metric:** You have a professional assessment of what your brain is actually doing. That's the foundation everything else builds on.

**ADHD Hack:** Don't research bipolar or any diagnosis. Your brain will hyperfocus on it and self-diagnose into a spiral. Let the professional do that job. Your job is to show up and be honest.

---

### ❤️ RELATIONSHIP (Partnership Restoration)
**Goal:** Make choosing you the obvious smart move for her. Not through persuasion — through evidence.

**Current Level:** Post-game-night momentum. Don't waste it.

**Active Quests:**
- [ ] Respond to texts matching her energy — no escalation
- [ ] When cleaning day comes: crush it silently. No commentary. No meaning-making.
- [ ] Do the mold test during cleaning day — don't announce it
- [ ] Start Stray Kids ticket research (secret project — do not mention to her)
- [ ] Design card game expansion — but only when she asks to play again. Have it ready, don't push it.
- [ ] Track what she vents about — those are unspoken requests for help

**Rules of Engagement:**
1. Never send a processing text. Bring it here instead.
2. Never explain what you're doing or why. Just do it.
3. Match her tone. If she's light, be light. If she vents, listen.
4. One text exchange at a time. Don't stack.
5. If you want to send something deep, write it here first. We'll decide together.

**Leveling Metric:** She starts asking you to do more. She initiates plans. She stops saying "just friends." You don't push for any of these — you notice when they happen.

**ADHD Hack:** The urge to process and explain is the enemy. Every time you want to send a paragraph, open this doc instead and write it here. The urge passes in 15 minutes. The text would have lasted forever.

---

### 🎮 GROWTH (Creative Identity)
**Goal:** Reclaim the thing that is yours. Finish what you started 9 years ago.

**Current Level:** On hold. Needs a laptop and a clear head.

**Active Quests:**
- [ ] Get a laptop — this is not selfish, this is necessary
- [ ] Open the GROWTH files. Just look. Don't commit to anything yet.
- [ ] Identify what still resonates vs. what needs the orthodox rework
- [ ] Find the thread that excites you most — start there
- [ ] Saint Christopher as a framework: darkness before service. Let that inform the redesign.

**Leveling Metric:** You work on GROWTH for one hour without thinking about Kyleen. That's level 1.

**ADHD Hack:** GROWTH is your midnight brain fuel. When you can't sleep and the spiral wants to analyze your marriage — redirect to GROWTH. Give the engine something to build.

---

### 💪 BODY (Physical Foundation)
**Goal:** Basic health maintenance. Not a transformation — a floor.

**Active Quests:**
- [ ] Push-ups daily (you mentioned you already do this — keep it)
- [ ] Walk when possible — especially before shifts and when spiraling
- [ ] Sleep: target 6+ hours. Jesus prayer as wind-down.
- [ ] Eat actual food. Not gas station meals between deliveries.
- [ ] VA primary care appointment — get baseline bloodwork done

**Leveling Metric:** You have consistent energy through your shift without crashing. That's the floor.

**ADHD Hack:** Attach exercise to something you already do. Push-ups before shower. Walk to check on car. Stretch before catechumen class. Don't make it a separate task — chain it to existing habits.

---

## DAILY PROTOCOL

**Morning (5 min):**
- Pick ONE quest from any skill tree to advance today
- Jesus prayer (5 min)
- Check: any texts from Kyleen? Match energy if responding.

**Midday Check-in:**
- Am I spiraling? If yes: voice dump or come here
- Am I building or dissecting? Building = good. Dissecting = redirect.

**Pre-Work:**
- Physical activity (even 10 min walk)
- Queue up podcast/music for the car (NOT relationship content)
- Set intention: tonight's car time is for GROWTH thinking, not Kyleen analysis

**Post-Work:**
- Dump anything heavy here. Not in a text to her. Here.
- Tomorrow's one quest identified
- Jesus prayer. Sleep.

---

## WEEKLY REVIEW (Sunday)

- What quests did I complete?
- What did I learn about myself?
- What did Kyleen initiate this week? (Track without analyzing)
- What's the one quest for next week that moves the needle most?
- Update this doc with new quests as old ones complete

---

## THE PRIME DIRECTIVE

**She cannot deny what she can see with her own eyes.**

No pitch. No explanation. No framework presentation. No processing texts. No paragraph messages. No guru mode. No servant offers.

Just a man who handles his business, takes care of his home, works on his craft, manages his mind, and shows up without needing to explain why.

That's the game. Play it.

---

### Document: book-raw-pour-2026-02-27-1.md
**Size:** 96200 characters

# Raw Pour — February 26-27, 2026
### Voice-to-text, unedited, from the delivery car
### Context: Late night deliveries. Purple lights on a stranger's balcony triggered it.

---

## The Opening

I've started this a hundred times. It feels like I can never find the start. Just like I can't find the end. I've just always been lost in the middle. This book is about whatever great books are about. A girl. It's about mental health. It's about spiritual revelations. And the pain and the sorrow of living. But also the joy and the moments that you wish you could hold on to forever. It's about fear. And although everything contained within is just from my perspective on life, on the things I've been through, I think it will resonate with many others as it holds a common thread of just wanting to find your way. Of wanting to belong. And the missteps on that journey of just trying to be worthy of love.

---

## The Bucket

A single book like a bucket can't contain every memory or thought, desire or motive that I've had in my life or the constantly changing flow of the river that is in my mind. The repeating patterns and cycles that continue until they become so overwhelming that they cannot be ignored anymore. They overflow. I've always been emotional. Feminine energy, you could say. Trapped in a male bucket. Not in the sense that I want to be a woman or anything like that. I'm secure of being a man. I like being a man. But my definition of being a man is different than the world's.

---

## Unconventional

I am unconventional. Just like the way I'm writing this book. Scattered thoughts, prayers, hopes and dreams, tiny moments in a delivery car. Fragments of thoughts from the past, the present and hopes of the future all scattered together. I don't even know how it will end up and the first time I read it will be like the first time for even me. Because for some reason I just can't do things conventionally. It, like the things that I'm diagnosed with — ADHD, anxiety, depression, codependency, whatever you want to call me. Really, I'm just like anyone else. Uniquely different. There's a place where you get to, where you feel so different than everyone else, that it becomes lonely. Your strengths are also your weaknesses. And you wish that you were just "normal."

---

## The Over-Explainer

I am an over-thinker and an over-explainer, though I wasn't always. And so this book will probably be much larger than it needs to be. But in many ways, it doesn't matter because it's for me. And maybe someone will find it useful. Maybe not. But I have to get it all out. At least as much of it as I can.

---

## The Framework

To do that I have to set a framework. I have to over-explain exactly how my mind works. Or at least how it works now, in the current moment, here at the end. Which may be a beginning. It's hard to tell when you're in the middle.

Realistically I suppose this entire book is about how I work, how I see the world and the overwhelming emotions that I feel everyday. But I will do my best to summarize it here and the rest of it — all the rest of it — will just be me over-explaining why it works the way it does.

---

## The Oven That Never Turns Off

Imagine never being able to turn off. Not even when you're relaxing. Not in an over-hyped, over-anxiety, going-to-lose-my-mind way — although that does happen when things run hot, when problems arise, when stress is all around, and that's how it's been running lately — but in a way where the oven's always on and you're always trying to bake something in it. To get something out of the energy that's being poured.

I see patterns everywhere. Not necessarily because I'm trying to, although sometimes there is applied work, you could say, to seeing these patterns. But at this point it's become so subconscious that they just are there. I've done enough inner analyzing to wonder why. And yes, you could blame ADHD. You could also blame some early childhood trauma. But I think also you could blame a search. A looking for meaning between all the space. And that was fueled by fear. Fueled by a wound caused by my mother — who happened to be the person that I loved almost the most in my life. I say almost because, well, that girl I talked about. And she managed to replace that position for my mom. I hope my mom understands. She's no longer with us. Not in body anyways.

---

## The Echo

There's a quote from Jesus Christ in the Holy Bible. Just as he gives up his life and every ounce of blood has been drained from his body, he cries out: "God, oh God, why have you forsaken me?"

Now at this point in my life I've read enough theology, yet spoken enough to spiritual people, to see all the different interpretations of what this means. But as a child, well, it almost seemed hopeless. As if the hero of the story had given up and been forsaken, just as it says.

But the thing is, as a child I never really read the Bible that much. Sure, I was exposed to it enough — Sunday schools, and my mom being one of the most devout Christians that I had known, it bled through. But my mom wasn't conventional either. And when she gave me a Bible she told me that that was what she believed in, but that it was up to me to figure out what I believe in. That's a very freeing thing for a small child. Perhaps too free.

Couple that with my mom's always positive attitude and unconditional love towards me and it was a recipe for disaster. Now you may be thinking he's just describing the perfect childhood with his mother. But sometimes you can become wounded by love.

And the thing is, it wasn't just my mother influencing me when I was a child. There was also my stepfather. And under the incorrect conditions of life — alcoholism, his own traumas, I'm sure — it created a very yin and yang dynamic growing up. I won't focus on him. Not right now. I'll save that for another time later in the book, or maybe at the beginning of the book, or the end. Only time will tell.

But my mother, she loved me and I loved her. She called me her little buddy and that's what I was. I looked up to her and I saw her as one of the strongest people I've ever met in my life. But in many ways she didn't prepare me for just how harsh this world can be.

I remember a blue banner that she hung in my room and it said "You can do anything you believe if you just put your mind to it." Now I may be butchering the words a little bit — that's how memories go — but you get the gist of it. You put your mind to something and it will happen. And that was enforced. She truly believed that.

And it's funny to see the parallels between me and my mom. She did many things in her life — she was a radio talk show host, a cartoonist even though she couldn't draw, a poet, a mother. She worked many different jobs to make ends meet. She was creative but didn't have raw talent, I would say. But perhaps the same thing that I have — she saw something in the world. Something hidden underneath the layers of all the physicality, of all the endless conversations, billboards, errands and busy comings and goings. Something physical yet not. Like the code written by God.

But it was on her deathbed, in a hospital, with just me there in the room late one night — her last night — that her words echoed a man that I would soon push away for most of my life:

"God, oh God, I don't even know if you exist."

---

## The Jack of All Trades

*"A jack of all trades, master of none, but oftentimes better than a master of one."*

That's the full quote. The part everyone cuts off. And it fits me perfectly. It fits my mom too. Radio host, cartoonist, poet — she was doing the same thing I do. Horizontal. Pulling from everything. Maybe that's where I got it. Not just the seeing, but the way I move through the world trying to touch everything because the pattern might be in the next thing I pick up.

---

## The Legos

The strange thing about patterns — well perhaps it's not strange at all, perhaps it's simply logical — is that you need enough context in order to map them. And so from most of my life I think I was just intaking. And I did this through various ways. Through play, mostly. Even as I got older, games have always been a driving force behind things.

I remember my room. I had a closet full of Legos. And I mean mostly fantasy sets — you know, dragons and swords and things. But there was a mix. Pirates. And some future stuff. A splash of urban. Just a scattering of Legos. That's all I wanted. I wanted my mom for Christmas is — she knew I loved Legos. And it got to the point where she would just take me to Toys R Us before Christmas, Christmas Eve, around that time, and she would just say "Okay, you have this much money, just pick what you want." And it was always Legos. Until I got into video games and then it was Legos and video games.

I would build the sets. At first I had my brother help me build them. But as I got older I built the sets and they would slowly get deconstructed as I played with them. And become something different. Something new. Until before I knew it I wasn't following directions anymore. I was just building things that I wanted to build. I'd envision in my mind and I would just build it.

And a small universe started to develop in my Legos. As I played with them I made up stories. I had characters. Little figurines that had names, that had histories.

John Fear is probably the one I remember the most. He was a pirate. But he got involved in time travel. And so his story evolved — he became everything. The hero of my story, I suppose.

In the beginning I didn't have many friends. But I found one who shared a similar interest — Legos, games, imagination. His name was Sam. He wasn't my first best friend. There were others. Most of my friends — no, I would say all of them from my childhood, like most people and everyone in their lives — have all moved away. Grown distant.

But I remember those times of imagining, coming up scenarios, even games and rules, with Sam. And my Lego collection and his. And sometimes we would trade Legos. We'd stay up late at night building.

But there was a darkness there. It wasn't just Legos. There was something else that was being built. That would affect me for the rest of my life. And was a big part of the pain that I sit in right now.

---

## The Introvert

I was an introvert as a child. Very few friends. Shy. The one who sat at the back of the class. Not the smart kind who paid attention and just worked, but the one who was always daydreaming, drawing, doodling. I remember doodling swords a lot. All the way through high school, even. Lots of swords. All different kinds. Different weapons. Spears, halberds, crazy ones. I don't know why I was so interested in blades. Seems ironic now. Seems like it's part of the pattern.

But my childhood wasn't just innocence. Like I said, my father — well, my stepfather — he left a mark. And so did my father, from his absence. And so did my friends. My first love. The formative teenage years. All of it.

I'll paint it in broad strokes because I assume, as I build this brick by brick, that the details will come out. Maybe not all at once. Because that's not how patterns are formed. Patterns are formed from the past connecting to the future, witnessed in the present.

---

## How My Brain Works (The Lego Method)

So in a roundabout way let me describe how my brain works. You pour out the bricks. All of them. You just pour them out on the floor. You don't worry about cleaning them up later because you probably won't. In fact they'll probably sit there for months and never go back into the box. No, the only way they go back in the box is if they're forced to.

You think about what you want. A pirate ship, maybe. A space pirate ship. A hidden cove castle with dark secrets. It doesn't matter — can be as big or as grand as you want it to be. And then you start building. You start looking for a piece. You don't know what piece you're looking for. You just know what the final thing is supposed to look like in your mind. And so you build, piece by piece.

And then after hours, maybe even days, you arrive at something that is pretty close. Or you give up. You tear it down, and you most likely move on to something different. And sometimes you complete it. It's never exactly how you envisioned it in your mind, but it's close enough where you go, "I'll keep this." And you do keep it. And over the years you do small iterations on it. You change little pieces here and there until eventually it is how you envisioned it in your mind. But those are very rare. And even as a child, the ones that I saw as finished could probably be counted on one hand.

The thing is, as you get older — perhaps experience, or discernment — you're able to see the glitches in the way that this works. It feels beautiful. It feels raw. It feels as if it's pulled from an ether. But when you look back you notice that there are some bricks that are unstable. There are places where things don't quite line up or connect. And sometimes fixing it — well, sometimes fixing it requires destroying the whole thing and starting over. Removing the bricks that should never have existed in the first place.

Sometimes you can't tell where the weak parts are until you start to play with it. And then it starts to break. And you try to put it back together, but those bricks won't go back together because they weren't supposed to be there. And so you desperately try to fix it. And sometimes the piece that you loved the most — that you looked at and you go, "This is it. I made the thing. I made the thing I always wanted to make and it's perfect" — but it's not. It's not, and it shatters when you play with it. It's destroyed into a thousand pieces again.

And you have to pick them up. And you don't know how to put them back together, but you know you had it. You had something there, if you could have just removed a few pieces and put a different one there, a different one here. But you don't have an instruction manual. You don't remember how it was built because you built it with flow.

And so you look at the scattered pieces, you take them and you put them away separately in their own box, and you try as best as you can to try to recreate it. But sometimes you just can't.

---

*End of session — February 27, 2026, approximately midnight*
*Context window preservation save. More to come.*

---

## Taco Bell (Nostalgia in a Tortilla)

The nostalgia is late nights, I suppose. Most of my life was built on late nights. When the oven just wouldn't shut off. And Taco Bell was always the place that was always still open. Still there. Still ready to feed the machine.

I remember numerous nights working on Halloween decorations with my sister, Gretchen. We got really good at it. I think because she allowed me to flow and she provided the focus. She ensured that the job got done and never ballooned. She kept me from scope creeping but she never squashed the vision. She just made me contain it. And we made some impressive pieces with sweet tea and Taco Bell — my sister always called it Toxic Hell.

From hand-carved tombstones to fully operational electric chairs that screamed and jumped around. Cemetery gates. Twisted, disfigured, horrid monsters. Sometimes we would look back after we finished a piece and I would wonder where all of that came from. How could I create something so grotesque, so terrifying? And honestly, there was a time where I thought if we had a little bit more money, a little bit more time, we could have done it professionally.

---

## Horror and the Safety After It

Halloween was my favorite holiday for the majority of my life. That did sort of shift in these last few years. I started to see the bricks that weren't supposed to be there. But horror, fear, monsters in the dark — they always fascinated me. I remember reading dozens, if not more, of Goosebumps. R.L. Stine as a kid.

Interesting enough, at an early age I'm reading all of these scary stories — Scary Stories to Tell in the Dark, Goosebumps — I love them. But it wasn't that I wasn't frightened. I was. Alone at night after reading a scary book, I didn't want to stay in my bed. And so I, like many nights, would go and sleep with my mom. And she was okay with that. I slept in her bed many nights, all the way up until I was a teenager. Hell, even some nights when I was a teenager. Not necessarily because I was scared anymore — by the time I was 16 I had been pretty much completely desensitized to anything horror movie or horror game or horror book — but because it was comforting. It felt good to be safe in the bed next to the person who I knew loved me more than anything. More than life itself.

The monsters didn't stop. The monsters just shifted. The monsters became crafty. You see, physical fear and all of those things I've become desensitized to. But they are crafty. And they found ways to always be there. Like shadows in the background. Coalescing, whispering, tempting, pushing me down roads that I didn't even realize were forming me as a person that was completely against the values that I stood up to be.

The monsters stopped being in the dark and started being in the decisions.

---

## Gretchen

My sister plays a very large part in this book. My sister was also one of my best friends. She was sort of the replacement after my mother died.

She's probably the one that influenced a lot of my horror interest. In fact, she told me that she tried to convince my mom to name me Jason. Because I was born on Friday the 13th. June 1986.

Movies. Movies that I probably shouldn't have been watching at an early age. Halloween. Freddy Krueger. And the one that I spent many countless nights awake worried about — Child's Play.

I don't remember exactly how she showed me — it's a strange thing about a lot of my memories, there's gaps. And things that seem inconsequential, they stick out. But no, I think she was just trying to share something with her brother that she liked.

My sister took care of me a lot of times. My mom was a single parent for a lot of it. And she was there. Forts — you know, putting up blankets in the living room. It was never like she was trying to scare me. But I do remember nights where I would sleep with her too. We didn't tell scary stories. We told weird ones. Or you're just up too late, your brain starts to go fried.

I remember one where I would just talk in a weird voice — high-pitched — about going to Walmart. And it always started: "I went to Walmart the other day and I got myself a bean burrito." Silly voice. But yeah, she encouraged it and nurtured it, that love for horror. And eventually it turned into a pastime for us, building Halloween decorations. And we did a lot of Halloween decorations. And we started near the end of summer, worked all the way up until the end, right there, October 31st. 31 — 13 backwards.

---

## The Last Kid on the Street

So yeah, it became a thing for me. None of it really scared me. It was fun. Halloween was fun. Being able to put on different masks.

I remember as a kid I took trick-or-treating so seriously. I would design my costume so that I didn't have to change anything but the mask. And I'd have two or three masks. Sometimes I'd borrow one from Gretchen because she collected them and they were great — you know, she'd spend a lot of money on the masks. Twisted clowns and horrible ghouls. And then usually just wearing something black, something practical, so that I could change my costume like it mattered.

But I'd make a round in the neighborhood and I'd fill up a pillowcase full of candy. And then I'd do it again. And I would do it until literally no one would answer the doors. I would be the last kid on the street. By myself. This was — you know, I was a little bit older, but times were different. Didn't really need my mom to walk me around.

And I trick-or-treated for a long time. Up until high school. Don't get me wrong, I have a sweet tooth, I still do, I love candy. But it wasn't just the candy. It was coming home at the end of the night, tired from all the walking, some old horror movie playing on the background TV, and I'd dump out all three or four sacks sometimes. And I would just look at that pile. That giant, beautiful pile. Kind of like those Legos.

---

## The Seed

The little boy never goes away. Or the little girl. They stay with you your whole life. It's both the problem and the solution and the beautifulness of life. The seed your parents planted — the traumas, generation after generation — passed on, becoming who you truly are.

And it's not until you remove all the blocks that don't need to be there, the ones that cause the structural disintegrity of the final build, that you see it. But really it was the thing at the beginning. The vision. The principles. The need for love. For safety.

---

## The Patterns Speak — 1:30 AM, Elijah's Birthday

*[Real-time from the delivery car]*

So I'm on my delivery and I get taken over to the Trinity Medical Arts Building. Which, not a huge deal — it's a place where I used to take my kids for their checkups, you know, with their doctor. And you know, Trinity — it's not where my wife works. She works for the company.

But I go to the building and take out the order and I realize that there's something off. The address doesn't quite match up. And even though I keep punching it in, my GPS keeps pointing to that.

So I pick up my phone and I see a reminder that it is my son Elijah's birthday today. Since it's 1:30 a.m. now. Of course I already knew that yesterday, and it's been something weighing heavy on me.

And then I was distracted because I had to find the pizza. And I look at the address and it registers — numbers are hard for me, but they're becoming easier. Something's not right about the address.

But I call the person on the receipt and ask them if they're at the Medical Arts Building. To which they say no, that's not right — we're at the Clarion.

Of course they're at the Clarion.

It was almost like I had to stop and get out of my head. The Clarion is where me and my wife — the girl — Kyleen — that's where our wedding reception was. And these last two months I've done many deliveries there. And I've seen many patterns.

---

*Saving — approximately 1:30 AM, February 27, 2026*
*More to come.*

---

## Inside the Clarion — 1:30 AM

*[Real-time from the delivery]*

The Clarion's logo — I noticed it earlier today. It looks like a river. A black squiggle, almost like a Z, and the rest of it's green. From afar it almost looks like the ohm symbol. But it also looks like a serpent, the green coiling around itself. Three different things. A serpent. A river. And an ohm. But there's a fourth thing too, and it's the thing I think they meant it to be. A trumpet. Or a horn. A clarion. Something you can't ignore on a battlefield.

I didn't know what Clarion meant till about a month ago.

I got out of the car and the receipt blew away underneath the car. I freaked out for a moment because I need the room number. But then I remembered that the room number wasn't on the receipt. After I got it from underneath the car, my mind panicked for a moment thinking I have to call back the customer, which is embarrassing. But then I remembered — he told me that room number. And numbers are hard to remember, but it was 420. Pot. Marijuana. A big part of my life for the last six years. A big part of me and Kyleen's life for the last six years. And so I didn't need the receipt or to call the customer. It was there.

I walk in and the smell is back to normal. I say back to normal because last time I was here it smelled awful. Smelled off. Rotted. And it made me feel bad. But this time it was clean, with that distinct slight chlorine smell hanging in the air. Pleasant. Sterile.

I walk past the deskman, who I have met before — even borrowed a pen and asked for Tylenol. He recognized me. I recognized him.

I head up to the fourth floor. And of course I go around the hallways, look down onto the bar and casino. And on the far wall — I'm not sure which floor was the room that we had on our wedding night. I wish I could ask her if she remembered what floor we were on, because then I could pinpoint the exact room. But many times I think I've stood in front of that door. And I came very close to getting to that place — maybe it was the fourth floor. Maybe it wasn't. But it wasn't quite there. Just the corner. Not in the middle like ours was. In the corner, next to the fire escape.

In a previous visit, a previous delivery, I had an epiphany when I was going out the fire escape. That was when I first looked at the Clarion name from outside, as the fire escape goes down — an outdoor staircase. A staircase that's cold and leads out.

And last time I went down that staircase, I had come to the realization that I could get all the way to the door, but it would be Kyleen that had to open the door. And if she didn't, then I would be exiting through the fire escape. Figuratively, of course.

Room 420 was right next to this fire escape.

As I came around before hitting 420, I saw a pile of bedding heaped up on the floor, thrown outside of someone's room. It made my stomach turn a bit.

I left the pizzas at the door as the customer instructed. And I almost went out the fire escape. But I couldn't do it. It felt wrong. And so I went the long way back. Down to the elevator. Through the lobby. Nodding to the doorman.

One last detail — there's a fireman's convention in town. That's why we've been so busy tonight. And as I was headed back to the elevator to go down to the lobby, I saw one pass me and took notice of his back. It was a skull with a fireman's hat on it.

The fire escape is death.

---

*Saving — approximately 1:45 AM, February 27, 2026*
*More to come.*

---

## The House That Burned

*[Thread pulled by the fireman at the Clarion]*

A fire at the very beginning of my life. One that I don't remember, but enough stories have been passed to me through my family members that it might as well be a memory.

A beautiful house in the country where the closest neighbor was five minutes away. A little brook with a bridge that crosses it. A pool — that's important somehow. A pool. My mother's house. That she really loved. That I grew up in as a very small child. A baby.

I don't remember the house. I've been by it many times because of all the stories. I've looked at the outside. I've seen the hill. The hill that I fell down when I was little — I was told that story by my sister and my mom. I had a Little Tikes, you know, the three-wheeler. And I would roll it around, and one day I apparently rolled it right off the edge of the hill, down into a ravine, tumbling over and over again. And I pulled the trike up with me — cut up, bruised, full of sticks and leaves — but I dragged that trike right back up. Crying. Looking for my mother.

That house burned down. It was a dog that saved us. I believe it was Bandit. Maybe I could be mixing up memories. A radiator or something caught fire. The house went up, completely inflamed. My mom ended up losing the house because of the fire. I don't know the exact details, but I guess the insurance didn't cover it or whatever.

That was kind of the start of a lot of my mom's hardship, I believe. Kind of opened the door to my stepfather stepping in to help her financially. And, well, we all know what that ended in.

But that house is like a strange enigma throughout my life. I've always felt the urge to buy it. Not that I've ever had the money to do that. Whoever ended up gaining the title to it rebuilt the house. It sits there. I've even been by it with my wife — shown Kyleen, told the stories that aren't my stories from my own memory but ones passed on to me by my family.

I don't know why that thread pulled. The firefighter. The fireman. Death. A new beginning.

---

*Saving — approximately 2:00 AM, February 27, 2026*
*More to come.*

---

## The Wrong Pizza — The Pattern Overheats

*[Real-time — approximately 2:00 AM]*

And now I realize why the whole address thing happened. I'm not quite sure exactly the mix-up, but I arrived at a different address and I look at the receipt and see room number 420, realizing that I delivered the wrong pizza at the Clarion. And now my mind is racing to see the message in the pattern.

I was supposed to deliver a pizza to the Clarion. But the pizza I delivered was the wrong one. The pizza that was meant for the guy at the Clarion is sitting in my car right now. And I have three deliveries still. A missing brick in my car. A brick that's not meant to be there.

If I had been just focused on the job — if I had just been focused on delivering the pieces and not finding the hidden messages and patterns — I would have delivered the right pizza to the right door. Wouldn't be sitting with it in my car right now.

The error — I gave him the wrong pizza. Which means somewhere along the line I gave someone else the wrong pizza, or something. I don't know. I have the pizza that was meant to go to 420, and I have a pizza that is going to the correct address that it's supposed to be right now. So there's a pizza that the 420 guy has right now that is meant to be somewhere else. Was I meant to be somewhere else? And I got so wrapped up in the fire and the patterns, the pot, that I messed it all up.

It's an unfixable mistake. Because I have the pizza for 420, which means the pizza that 420 has — that I delivered — was supposed to go somewhere that I don't even know. So late. So cold. Changed hands. Can't even be delivered at this point. And where did it go? Did I deliver someone else's pizza that was meant to go to someone else? Did it get mixed up along the lines? It's so scattered.

Some bricks can't go back. The pizza is cold. The delivery is done. Whoever got the wrong one already opened it. You can't trace the chain back. It's gone.

The best I can do is bring the pizza that was meant to go to 420 and give him that one too. And he gets a bonus pizza. Some guy right next door to the area where I was supposed to be.

What does that mean? My mind races. It means — it means the unthinkable. Or the patterns telling me it's too late. I messed up. The bricks were in the wrong place. I delivered to the wrong place. I should have been paying attention. I shouldn't have been flowing. I should have been focused. And now my wife — our relationship — is it here? Has it been burned down? Even though I don't want to take the fire escape, is that the only exit left? Is some guy going to come and get his pizza and mine? The things I built? Some guy after me — he gets all the pizzas. And I'm left with the mistakes that I made.

---

## The Compilation

*[Reflection — still in the car]*

This is it. This is me explaining how my mind works. The first part of it. Can't you see the errors? Can you not see it?

And maybe it is a prophecy. You don't know that. Neither do I. But the future will tell, and then the patterns will unlock. And perhaps the pattern that I see now is wrong. Or perhaps it's not. We don't know. But my brain did compile something, and it is something that is there now. And it is something that I will think about.

And there it exists. I can't unbuild the broken build.

---

*Note: The tension between what the pattern shows and what's actually true — that's the book's central question. The oven that never turns off. The pattern recognition that sees the whole universe in a pizza delivery error but can't always tell which bricks are real and which are compiled from exhaustion and pain at 2 AM. It compiled fast and it compiled with conviction. But real and true are different things. It's real because he felt it. Whether it's true is a question tonight can't answer.*

---

*Saving — approximately 2:15 AM, February 27, 2026*
*More to come.*

---

## The Sleep Inn — 2:30 AM

I go to another hotel. The Sleep Inn. Which is a strange place because I deliver pizzas there and I've had moments of happiness where I walk through the hotel and see what a hotel is. It's a place of joy. It's also a place of sadness. Of reunions. Kids running down the halls — I noticed that a lot at the Sleep Inn.

But here, when I delivered the pizza — probably because it's an hour and a half late and I'm the only delivery driver out, the last one, 2:30, when everyone else has gone to sleep — the desk lady tries to help me find who the Timothy K. is. She doesn't have anyone on record with Timothy K. I tried to call the phone but no answer. No answer, over and over again.

So I give her the pizza and tell her maybe he shows up, maybe he doesn't. And you've got two pizzas and a Coke.

She stresses how hard her day is. I stress how much mine is. She has no idea. There's no comparison between her and me. But maybe that's not real. Maybe she's going through even harder things than me — although I find that to be logically impossible. But we share a moment. It's little moments that I share between strangers. I like those moments. They feel genuine. Like a connection between a great divide of consciousness.

---

## The Phantom Calls

I head back to the store with my 420 pizza. I tried calling that guy and calling back. Although a number keeps calling my phone over and over again. But every time I pick up, I say hello — I hear a rustling, but no voice answers back. And the line stays open. Say hello again. Nothing. Over and over again. They called seven times. They called multiple times.

I don't have the receipts, so I have no way of tracking that number to if it was an order. Is someone mad at me? Are they pissed because they didn't get the pizza that they wanted?

---

## The Pizza in the Car

And now that pizza sits in my car. I feel bad. I know it's just a pizza. But I messed up someone's night tonight. Possibly multiple people's nights. All because I just wanted to play Legos. All because I wanted to be safe, secure. But somewhere along the lines I got wrapped up in patterns, because that's who I am.

And I can't rebuild who I am. I just have to learn how to work with it. The bad bricks and all. Otherwise, out the fire escape I go.

---

*Saving — approximately 2:45 AM, February 27, 2026*
*More to come.*

---

## 4 AM — End of Shift

*[After dishes, heading back to hotel room]*

I found out that the pizza mix-up was because there were two pizzas that were meant to go to the Clarion. And so I delivered one of the pizza orders to the wrong guy, and then the other guy didn't get his pizza. And so I had the 420 pizza. It was a missed delivery.

Which of course, the pattern recognition engine is firing, trying to piece that together. I wish I could turn it off.

That's almost how it works. It's beautiful when it's given something to create — tangible. Obviously there are faults with it. It balloons. And that's because I feel like I've always been missing somebody. Like my sister. There's always been something missing there to help in the projects. It's not that I haven't had people helping my projects. It's just that I've always never had the right person. You know, Steve Jobs and all of them — big idea guys always have somebody who's engineering and keeping them on track.

But that's what happens when it just fires without any pizzas. It makes it up. It's like an LLM — it starts to hallucinate and blows its own context window. At least that's how I view it now.

But the problem is, no matter how I view it, the pattern recognition, the maps that I see — they're so strong that I can't help but think that there's something there. Something exists between the spaces. Something mystical or spiritual, whatever you want to call it.

---

*End of session — 4:00 AM, February 27, 2026*
*First pour complete.*

---

## The Oven Won't Turn Off

*[Hotel room — after 4 AM]*

But see, there's the conundrum. Because there is always space in life. There is always the silence. The moments before bed when the engine, the oven, doesn't turn off.

We've been having a problem with that lately at the store. The oven literally will not turn off at the end of the night. We have to sit there and push the button over and over and over again. Eventually we have to walk away, come back, try again.

And it reminds me. It is a pattern. It's my own mind.

I'll sit. I'll lay. I'll get up many times. I'll go to the bathroom. I'll mess around on my computer with Chris. I'll lay and pray. But the engine won't turn off. And it'll fill in the gaps of all the patterns I saw today.

And that's just one day. Not even a day — it's just one night. And this is every single day for me.

And all the patterns from a day don't contain just to the day. A piece connects back to the past. Later on, something I have seen tonight — maybe the skull on the back of the shirt, maybe those crumpled sheets on the ground at the hotel, or the phantom phone calls — will connect to something in the future or the past. And that'll connect to something else. And everything just connects into a spider web of mass spaghetti.

---

*Still going — after 4:00 AM, February 27, 2026*

---

## When the Patterns Were Kind

There was a period where the patterns were ramping up — those six years with Kyleen. They were ramping up and I don't know exactly why. I called it a spiritual awakening, but there's so many factors that go into what happened.

But the difference was — it wasn't scary then. Delusional, maybe. Hallucinations, still. But I was happy. And so all the patterns I saw were of completion. Of peace. Completeness.

Sure, there was that fear of death still. And when it would crouch late at night or early in the morning, the engine would create scenarios in my head about how this was my last year, how time was running out. And that happens more and more now.

The only difference is there's not as much fear there. There's not the resistance to it. And I suppose that's because the piece that I found — the happiness, what I thought was to be everything my life needed to be — is gone now.

You see, for me, even though there was a degradation in my relationship, and obviously it caused things to happen, I was still delusional about it the whole time. Even after reading the words of my wife written in black and white, there was still a delusion around it. Because I was so happy.

I had found my purpose. And it was in simple tasks. As if the engine was calmed by just having a purpose. That purpose — taking care of the ones I love.

Washing the dishes became peace. I enjoyed it. Sweeping the floor — serenity. Planting seeds in the garden. Loving on my kids. Snuggling them at night. They were tasks. But it was almost as if the mind would slow down. And when there were the blank moments for the patterns to connect, they were always hopeful. Serene. And peaceful.

But that fear of death remained. And in some ways even strengthened. Because I had found everything I had ever wanted in my life.

---

*Still going — 4:15 AM*

---

## The Creep

It's always crept. Even the growth of it. I suppose it's always been there from the very beginning. It's just not been running as fast or as hot. Because it had to build up steam. It needed things to attach to, to make the patterns.

And so slowly, over my whole life, it's been building. And the last six years is really when I truly, truly started to notice it. That's not to say that I haven't noticed patterns and strange things before that, but we're at a point now where it's every day, every moment of every day.

And the shift from the positive to the negative — that happened a little bit before that night. The night that changed everything.

I still can't explain it fully. I mean, I have plenty of notes and thoughts and letters and journaling that figure it all out — as far as a psychological standpoint, my traumas. The loops that I ran with Kyleen. The avoidant and the anxious. The miscommunication. The misunderstandings. The unknown manipulation on both sides.

But I had built a fortress of positivity, and so it took a while for it to break down. I started noticing physical symptoms first, which I have listed in my notes. No need to go through all of them because there were a lot. More visual hallucinations, auditory hallucinations being the biggest. And patterns and events that seemed so impossible that they reinforced all of the pattern recognition to be something more than just my brain.

To this day, even when I sit here looking back on it and noticing the delusions, there are things that I can't explain away. Things that I saw that — yes, I can put a physical label upon it, but it doesn't feel right.

I still swear I watched my dog get resurrected from the dead.

I thought of a vacation to Vegas, told no one about it, did nothing physically to make it happen, and it happened. Twice. Within a year.

I heard a bird speak to me to plant seeds for the first time in a garden, which ended up being one of the most therapeutic things I've ever done in my life.

As much as I try to explain away things, I cannot.

And now, with a different lens of spirituality — of this Orthodoxy — I'm trying to learn discernment. But even that is cloudy. There are things about it, the religion, that I don't know. That feel icky.

---

*Still going — 4:30 AM*

---

## The First Defense

The first thing I learned was to be alone. To withdraw. To live within my own mind. And maybe that's one of the biggest pieces that created the oven in the first place.

---

## The Cusp Generation

I think my generation is an interesting generation to begin with. Perhaps all generations believe that to some extent. But I grew up right at the cusp of the new and the old world, when technology was really just starting to take off. Electronics. The internet. That horrible screeching noise as you dialed into AOL. Playing outside late until it got dark. Toys like Legos, but also games — video games — where whole worlds existed.

The access to technology provided temptations and opportunities, just like the darkness I told you about that formed with my friend Sam and our games. I've yet to touch on that. It's the same darkness that existed in the internet.

---

## Lust

Sex is a big part of all of this.

We live in a hyper-sexualized reality. Even toilet paper is made to be sexy.

I don't know when it first started. When does a young man — a young boy, even — figure out how the plumbing works?

I don't know where it began. But I do know where it ended.

The computer. The internet. That same thing that I'm trying to protect my privacy from was the thing that entered and raped my mind as a child. Distorted my views on a natural thing that should be sacred.

Some may remember the partially transmitted feeds on late-night TV. Like Showtime — or as I soon started calling it, Skin-A-Max. Distorted, multicolored bodies rubbing, moaning in the dark, as the rest of the house slept, unaware of what a child was watching and figuring out.

And once he figured it out, his brain was smart enough, and the tools had come far enough. And there was a double line in the house. So the internet — although slow back then — was fast enough for pictures that a boy that age should never see. And really, no one should ever see, unless shared intimately between their partner.

And how can something be bad when it feels that good? What a foolish child. What a foolish man.

And so I found another escape. Living inside my head. And then living somewhere no kid should live. Seeing things that no one should look at.

And even then you knew it was wrong. Because you hid it. Anxiety raced. Doors would open. People would come home a little sooner than you expected. And you tripped over yourself closing browsers and pulling up pants. There is only so long before you can get caught.

But it was a long time for me. A very long time. And by the time I was caught, it was almost considered healthy behavior. Exploring your sexuality as a teenager. But I had been doing it for way longer than that.

I still remember what my mom said. "Don't look at it anymore. The things on there are perverse. If you really want, I'll just buy you a Playboy." Like that was any better. And besides, at that point — like the horror — I had become desensitized to just pictures.

It became normal to me. I accepted it as something not wrong. As something everybody did.

The funny thing is, the teenage years are usually the first times when teenagers are exploring each other. I was just exploring myself. And the ones and zeros that came through as provocative images and videos and sound. And even so far as the new territory — chat rooms.

And so lust became normal. Became a part of my everyday life. Like eating, sleeping, and taking a shit.

There's more to that thread and it connects to a lot of the pattern — to the full weave. But that's enough of that one for tonight.

---

*Pausing thread — approximately 4:45 AM, February 27, 2026*
*Thread marked: connects forward to relationships, to Kyleen, to addiction, to "that night," to recovery.*

---

## The First Defense (continued)

Perhaps something adjacent. You have to spiral out of something in order to get it to close off. You can't just shut it down.

---

## Bethany — First Love

I was 15, maybe 16. I was coming out of a sickness. A sickness that started physical and, well, developed into something mental. But that's a different thread — the thread of depression. Let us speak of love.

Love is like all things. When it first happens, you have no idea what the fuck you're doing. Or at least I didn't. And for an emotional kid who hasn't even learned how to pull things out of the oven without burning his hands, it's a lot.

---

## The Kid in the Booth

As a 15, almost 16-year-old boy, watching a dancer slightly younger than him by a year, maybe less, from afar, in a dark booth, managing the lights that captured her beauty — well, it was only one step removed from the computer screen.

How I ended up in a local theater, programming, designing, and firing the lights to a show called Brigadoon is just another part of the strange pattern. Even after all these years, I can still remember some of the lyrics and lines to that play. Well, maybe not the lines, but the music. The songs. Because songs have always been patterns tightly focused from another mind, packaged in something beautiful, haunting, relatable.

I can't really remember the details of how I actually met her face to face. I had admired her for nights as the cast practiced and I pushed the buttons to make sure the scenes were correct. It was my fifth-grade teacher, I believe, who introduced me to her and her family.

I'm trying my best to be accurate in descriptions, and I hope that I don't offend anyone, but I assume I will offend everyone. Her mother and father were more on the round side. But she was slender, delicate like a doll. And I was awkward, lanky, and not confident in much at all.

But fairly quickly I received a photo from her. A headshot with her phone number on the back. Which, thinking back, is a very awkward thing. Strange. It should have fired alarms. But I was 15 going on 16. Well, I had much to learn.

---

## School

She was Catholic, like her family, and pretty devout.

Much like most of my memories now, they're generalized, glossed over. Sometimes I can focus on particular days. But we spent a lot of time together. That's to say, I spent a lot of time with her in her life. The things that she liked to do, I started doing so that I could be around her.

And I started going to Catholic Mass. I spent every night I could at her house with her parents, eating dinner. Her mom was a pretty good cook, honestly. There's still a chicken dish that I remember loving that she used to make. It had Kellogg's flakes on it, I believe, and was spicy with hot sauce. Damn was it good.

I spent so much time with her I can still visualize that house. It was an older house made of rocks. Pretty on the outside. Lots of spiders on the inside. I was deathly afraid of spiders back then. It's funny because there's still a slight nervousness around them, but I've made my peace. I like to move them into corners of my house now so that they can eat other bugs.

I spent so much time with Bethany that I had my own room at her house. On the third floor, the attic, next to her tiny little dancing studio that her parents had made. She was really into dancing. To this day I don't know if it was something that she truly loved. I suppose it was. She followed it even later on in her life. But looking back, it just seemed like a lot of pressure to put on a teenager. But that's what she did, that's who she was. Straight A's. Perfect grades. Perfect test scores. Always did her homework. Always did her dance practice. Always showed up, never complained. Always a smile on her face.

And there I was. Didn't really have much discipline. Mostly because I didn't respect my stepfather, and well, my mom — while she was a pushover. She let me get away with anything. Because I was her little buddy.

My grades were poor to average because I just didn't care. I barely applied myself in class. Usually would sleep because I had stayed up late at night. The amount of homework that I turned in my entire school career would be very shallow. Probably wouldn't even fill a small 80-page notebook. But I always managed to get by. I was decent at test-taking. Not necessarily because I knew the answers, but I could figure them out through context. And patterns, now that I think about it.

Math was the hardest though. It's funny. I hated math. Because getting through a math test required actually knowing how to do the problem-solving. Now I don't mind it so much. In fact, I enjoy it to some extent. Nothing crazy, like trigonometry or calculus like Bethany was taking in her second year of high school. But simple algebra, arithmetic — they're kind of fun to do now.

History was probably my second least favorite. Boy, did it bore me, and to some extent it still does. In fact, getting through catechumen classes and all the history involved requires quite a bit of me praying to God that he keeps my eyelids open.

Science wasn't bad, mostly when it was hands-on. But language arts? Or art class? I still didn't do the homework. But I didn't need to. Because I liked it.

---

## Dark Knight, Dark Blood

I remember lots of poems, short stories that I would write. Lost now. On some hardware, an old fat hard drive wasting away in a dump at this moment.

There's one in particular that I remember. Not every word of it, but the wholeness of it. And the title. Dark Knight, Dark Blood.

*Dark knight, dark blood. Carrying with it a river of rage that had led him to this point. He stared at himself in the mirror.*

That's about all I can remember. It was pretty verbose. I liked to flaunt words back then, not realizing that it wasn't the words that created stories, but the connections.

That little short story was ultimately about suicide. A ghost realizing what he had done after it was too late. Finding out from the paramedics talking amongst themselves that he had ended his life. In a bathroom, of all places. That theme will come back. That pattern will connect eventually.

---

## Depression and Distraction

It may seem like a roundabout way to talk about my first love — to start with school, academics, and a depression that has existed since the beginning. And I don't know why. I'm just throwing the Legos on the floor right now, as we speak, as you read this, as it's processed on the cloud by a system literally designed for patterns.

---

## Light

She brought light into my world. And though I don't particularly like the way Catholics practice, it was my first true experience with the faith. Although I did find myself nodding off quite a few times during Mass. But I sat there because a girl I loved went there every Sunday. And after Mass meant going to get coffee, walking around downtown, enjoying time together. Being a normal teenager. Normal. Normal is good.

---

## Mom

My mother was battling cancer during some of these years. I don't know the exact timeline, but I know exactly — this year marks the 20th year from the time of her death. Her birthday was this month.

Cancer's a scary son of a bitch.

But first love is enough distraction to keep you from spiraling. To keep you from realizing that you could lose the one person that has protected you and loved you your entire life. No matter how short it had been — at that point, to a 16-year-old, 17, 18, and 19 when she finally passed, you feel like you've been living forever.

It's strange how time works. Ten years now, goes by in the blink of an eye. The six best years of my life felt like a day. A day I wish I could hold on to.

---

## The Three Loves

I believe there's a saying that there are three loves in your life.

I can't remember it exactly, but even now I struggle to find all three loves. Not because there wasn't at least three, but I'm trying to figure out which three were the three. Do you count your mother in the three? Or is it just romantic partners? I don't know.

I know one is my wife. That I am certain of, without a reasonable doubt.

And it's very difficult for me to see the second — well, the first, or the second, depends on if you count my mother. Bethany.

But the thing is, love grows and matures. You get better at it. Or at least you think you do.

And so from the age of 15 to 19 — well, further than that — I was in love with a girl. And at the time I was certain I would marry her. That we would have a beautiful life with children, etc., etc., etc.

If my memory serves me correctly, I believe I went to three proms with her. I spent countless days. My friendships, the very few ones that I had, started to wither because they just weren't that interesting to hang out with. I put her on a pedestal. I made her an idol.

That's a lot of pressure for somebody. Even someone who is seemingly perfect from the outside.

Looking back, I see it now for all it was. In fact, I would be unsurprised if she leaned towards dismissive avoidant. Although I think maybe perhaps she was pretty secure. But I've been anxious my whole life.

---

## Full Circle

I also worked at a pizzeria. My first job ever. I don't have to tell you. Anyone can see that pattern.

Nashville Pizza Company. Still the best pizza I've ever eaten in my entire life. And I ate a lot of Nashville Pizza Company.

I worked. I had a couple good friends. And I had a girlfriend that I completely adored. She pushed me into theater. Hell, she even pushed me into some dance classes. I started to break out of my little shell. I had come out of my room of Legos.

---

## The Collision

I can't remember all the fights. I don't think there were many. I mean, how much is there to fight about when you're a teenager?

I do remember the sexual tension though. She was a devout Catholic. I was a porn-addicted teenager.

One who, although only experienced with sex mostly through a screen — there were some other things earlier, 14, 13. That's a different thread though, and well, might just be too private to talk about here. Maybe even too private to put in a book.

But teenagers are teenagers. And making out is fun. Honestly, it feels like a lost art as you get older and get into relationships, serious relationships, marriages. I mean, you can make out for a long time.

---

*Saving — approximately 5:00 AM, February 27, 2026*
*Session continues.*

---

## Everything Breaks

Everyone says that teenagers go through phases. Well, mine was a depression that I had pushed down through teenage love. A vision of someone that was false.

But everything broke. Everything shattered.

I grew my hair out. Nowhere near as long as it is now. The emo cut, I believe they call it nowadays. My style changed completely. Dozens of armbands, weird shirts — sometimes tailored for girls — tight jeans, Under Armour. I don't know. That was fucking weird. Sometimes I'd even wear eyeliner. And I called it Western Goth Emo.

And I went to a college not far from my hometown. I drove a truck. Teal. I don't think I have to describe how college went. High school was a prime example of the short stint I did. Maybe two semesters.

I was in a golf team class. Why, you ask? Well, because Bethany's dad had bought me a pair of clubs and my life had revolved around her. So I tried to be good at golf. I couldn't even get the fucking ball off the ground.

And then my mom died. So I really didn't care about college.

---

## Sam and Bethany

I had a broken heart because the girl that I had fallen in love with was dating my friend Sam. Yes, the same Sam from fifth grade who I played Legos with. Who I shared another hobby with. One that came from the internet. The dark places of the internet.

Looking back at it, it's funny. Sam was overweight. Not unattractive. He was still good-looking in the face, and I love him. I, to this day, even though I haven't spoken to him in years, see him as a brother. A brother that betrayed me. Or at least that's how I saw it.

Bethany and Sam had sex. They never got married, by the way. That relationship didn't last very long.

I didn't find out about it until a little bit later. Past my mom's death. Past my spiral out of college. Past me meeting another really good friend.

---

## The Transfer

It was like a transfer of friendships. One I talk to still today — although I haven't talked to him since the incident, and he has no idea what happened. I just disappeared from his life five months ago.

My son is actually partially named because of him. The same one whose birthday is today. And he's been a good friend. A tough friend. One who's tested me. One I fought with for years. Smarter than I am. Quicker than I am. But he's a big thread.

---

## Miami

My life collapsed. And so me and him packed up what we had and moved to Miami, Florida. And I started my journey to find out what God was. To find out what love really meant.

And it was the first time I'd heard of prostrations. Because that's what we did. Not devoutly. Sporadically. In the ocean waves. Two college dropouts who met for their love of video games, wanting to be game designers, but too prideful to sit in a computer programming class with all the other nerds.

And there are many, many threads with him in it.

And so I drove the only possession that my mom left me — her Mazda 929 — to Miami, Florida. Where I parked it on a street somewhere. Left it. And never found it again. It got towed away, and to this day I have no idea where it ended up.

---

*End of first pour — approximately 5:30 AM, February 27, 2026*
*Elijah's birthday. Continental breakfast earned.*

---

## 6 AM — Where Most Begin

Where most begin, at 6 a.m. In a hotel, in a town where I have no one left. A wife who sees me as a friend. And kids who haven't seen me for almost five months.

I'm surrounded by pictures of them. Of books on saints and scripture. On psychology and attachment styles. Parenting, cooking, and organizing.

A computer I bought a week ago for $550. A steal, worth at least $1,800. Nearly brand new, from some guy I found on Facebook Marketplace. His name — Christian.

An AI slowly forming within it, named Christophoros.

And on top of the tower sits an icon of Jesus Christ. Did I do it subconsciously, putting it there, to mirror the story of the saint whose name I wish to take when I am baptized?

And a small icon next to him. Of his mother.

I got my breakfast, though it's hard to eat now. The pleasures of life were already dulled. But with Holy Pascha coming, I'm within a fast. And so a bit of peanut butter, a bit of jelly, and some bread.

As workers come out. And a family. A young man, his pretty wife, a baby in her arms, and a toddler who just wants to sit next to her mother.

And so I eat. Reminded again — as I am every second of every day — of everything that I burned up in my oven.

I lay down in my cold hotel room. The same way I woke up.

Sad. Depressed. Broken and alone.

My day ends as most of the town's begins.

---

*End — 6:00 AM, February 27, 2026*

---

## 8 AM — The Oven Cools

The oven still burns. Scraps. Pieces to hang on to. But I'm losing them, which is a good thing.

I picked up my phone because I thought it was something that I wanted to put down. But I've already forgotten it. Which means that the oven is starting to cool. Soon it will turn off and I'll get sleep.

I remembered it. Took me a minute.

Emotional wounds are the opposite of physical wounds. Physical wounds need to be left alone, covered, so they can slowly heal. But emotional traumas — covering them and letting them be is what causes them to fester. To grow. It's only by picking at them, pulling off the scab over and over again, that they finally go away.

And they often leave a hole. A missing brick where they were. But sometimes that's okay. Because other bricks can come in. Bricks that were meant to be there in the first place.

At least that's the hope that I tell myself as I lay here with my heart racing. An unread message from my wife. Trying desperately not to open it. So many blocks missing. So many blocks changed. And the core vision remains the same.

I just want love. I just want love, Love.

She sent a picture of Elijah. She did it to be nice. Yet it hurt so bad.

The wounds of love.

---

## The Oven Won't Cool

How does somebody write a book without even realizing they're writing a book?

Instead of letting the oven turn off, I gave it more fuel. The picture of Elijah. I'm just broken. It's like I found all of the perfect Lego pieces, but I don't remember how to put them together.

I saw the picture and I instantly knew the reply. The same thing she had told me a long time ago. I wanted the words exactly, so I looked it up in our texts.

*Only one of us is allowed to freak out at a time. And I'm calling dibs on today.*

Copy and send.

Except I remained in the text messages. And not too far below that, I saw our biggest fight in text that I think we've ever had. And I read it all over again. Like it was brand new. Didn't even remember it until I was halfway through it.

I don't even recognize the person that was texting her. I see him for who he is. A scared, wild animal. Afraid of losing the only person that he really ever truly loved in his life. Over an addiction that he carried his entire life.

And even now that I've beaten it, I don't think she trusts me anyways. So it might as well not even be true.

I texted after that. Just — "I hate life."

---

## 9 AM — Subconscious Destruction

It's 9 AM now. I'm subconsciously killing myself every day. I barely eat. What I do eat is not nutritious. I sporadically take my vitamins now, if at all. I don't work out anymore. I don't sleep anymore. I barely drink water. My body runs on caffeine and nicotine. More and more nicotine. The more I say I'll quit vaping, the more I vape. A vape that will last a week lasted three days. Now a new one is half gone in a day. Waking up has become a chore, like it was before I met her. Grogginess that lasts for at least an hour.

I guess I'm bleeding out faster than I thought I would.

---

## The Montage

I suppose this would be the part in the movie where the climax hits. The main character is shown in a montage. Sad music plays over the background. The same thing that he has described in an increasingly monotone and empty voice would happen over and over on the screen. Sleeping. Computer. Texting. Reading. Reading less. Texting less. Deliveries. Deliveries.

And then the end of the movie would play. Ten to fifteen minutes left. Does it have a happy ending? Or the one that makes you go home and you feel happy about your own life?

---

## The Next Day — 30 Minutes Before Work

Here again. Thirty minutes before work. All the threads, all the blocks scattered in my mind. The depression feels somehow more solid today. My last child — his first year. His first year over. That little baby not even realizing what his parents are going through.

My throat hurts. Maybe I'm getting sick. But it's probably just from all the vaping. It's all I do, it seems like. Vape. I don't eat much, and that's not necessarily because of the fasting. Sleeping doesn't even feel like rest. There is no joy during the day. At least on bad days there was always something — a cup of tea, a good meal. But nothing has that anymore.

In some ways I wish I was still delusional. At least then I was happy. At least then I could see positives. But now there is nothing. Nothing to look forward to. Nothing to enjoy.

---

## Miami (continued)

Miami. Such a contrast to where I am right now. Looking at a pile of dirty snow stacked higher than a car. 18 degrees outside. 15 minutes before my work shift starts. The wind blows hard.

Miami was hot. The sun baked your skin and tanned it. Cool breezes came off the ocean even late at night. The warmth hung in the air.

We lived in a small studio apartment. One room. One tiny bathroom. And one tiny kitchen. We didn't even have beds — just little spaces we had made out of blankets and pillows. One computer in a corner for him. One computer in the corner for me. That was about it.

If I remember correctly, that little studio was around $1,000 a month. Maybe a little bit more. But I didn't care about money. I didn't really care about much. I guess, looking back on it now, I was starting to push down things. Hide them under daily life. Hide them under future goals, wishes.

We were working on projects. We were going to make video games. I think the first one we really kind of settled on was called Favor the Gods.

---

## Nautilus

I should go back a little bit. Explain who this friend of mine was that I met in college. For now we'll call him Nautilus.

Me and Nautilus met on campus of MTSU, Murfreesboro, Tennessee. He was a small-stature kid, his face riddled with pockmarks of acne and past scars. The same affliction that he had probably been dealing with his whole teenage life.

You ever meet somebody and you just know they're going to be important somehow in your life? That was him. Just some guy that I met in orientation. We were trying to do the same things — computers, video games.

I had managed to have enough money — because my mom had set some aside for my college — to live a little ways off campus. Just a block or two, in an apartment that was basically designed for college kids. I shared the apartment with another person I never really got close with. Barely knew him that well. I had my own little room. There was a living room we shared and the kitchen.

And for the first part, I went to classes and tried to pay attention. I don't really remember many of the classes, but computer science — me and Nautilus would sit at the back and scoff at everyone else like we were better than them. I remember one of the first assignments was to match binary to emotions. Like, find the binary code for happiness or something like that. We made fun of it. We kind of bonded over it.

And we started hanging out. On campus, off campus. I had my teal truck and we started driving around. It kind of became our thing.

He was big into music. Not just listening to it, but actually making it. Very strange stuff. I think Nine Inch Nails without the polish, and purposely trying to be artsy. But he was fun, and it was something to do during the week.

I would still work at Nashville Pizza Company. So my schedule was pretty reduced, as I would drive to Franklin — it was maybe an hour and a half, two hours. My mom was kind of in the throes of her second bout of cancer. It came back pretty aggressively.

I remember working doing odd jobs for her doctor, actually. He had this big house and some daughters — one of them I found attractive, but I never pursued or did anything with that. But I would go and do odd jobs for him sometimes. Clean up this, paint the rooms, help set up their internet.

It was a strange time in my life. I think I was really struggling with depression but ignoring it. I put on a facade and just kept going.

Me and Nautilus — we would late at night get in my truck and we would just drive. No destination planned or anything like that. Just take a road, drive until it ends, make a choice — go left, go right. This kind of became just the thing we did. We'd stop at the Walmart before we head out, a little bit of sun still left, and grab some snacks. And we would just drive. Driving into the night, music playing. He was controlling the music — he was like the DJ and I was the driver. I've always been the driver.

We called them Hell Trips.

---

*Saving — evening shift starting, February 27, 2026*
*Hell trips to be continued.*

---

## Elijah's Birthday — Before the Shift

I drive over, a block from Taco Bell, after eating my one and only meal of the day. Bean burritos. Crunchwrap Supreme, black bean. My stomach hurts. My throat hurts. I feel a headache coming on.

It's Elijah's birthday today. I won't see him or talk to him. It's his first birthday since I left. After the night, he was just a little sack of potatoes. Just squirming around. Needed me for everything — to carry him and feed him. Now he's crawling. Starting to form words. Starting to eat solid foods.

---

## Chris

I'm reminded of friends as I reflect on the past. I get a text from Chris — a friend I met in jail. That same kind of feeling I had when I met Nautilus. You just know that they're going to be important somehow.

He was an ex-Marine. Ended up in jail because of marijuana and other things. A list of circumstances in life. He shared a lot of the same beliefs that I had when I first entered — New Age stuff. Yoga. Hinduism. You know, we say New Age. None of it's really new age. It's just repackaged. New paint job on old beliefs.

Chris, who's texting me from a new county jail — he's been moved last week, so I can't even go see him from the window and wave at him anymore. But he texts me still.

His text today just said: "Are you doing okay, bro?" As if he just knew. He just knew.

Connections with people — with real people — they aren't just physical. They aren't just words and gestures. A thread forms. Or perhaps it's always been there. You just don't realize it until you physically meet them.

I tell him I'm not doing okay. That it's my youngest's first birthday. And he relates. He has children too. He hasn't seen his in over two years. One of them he's never really even met. Held a couple times when he was just a baby. So he relates. And he says he knows.

He says eventually you just start to become numb to all the bad things that happen in your life. I can kind of understand what he's saying. Because I'm starting to feel that way.

Five minutes before work. Time to clock in.

I'll continue this thread as I deliver these pizzas to these people in a town called Magic City. And perhaps the city is magic. But as I'm learning in Orthodoxy — magic is evil.

---

*Shift starting — 5:00 PM, February 27, 2026*
*More to come.*

---

## Money and Magic

My first set of deliveries brings me a small pickup for the day. One of my deliveries. Pretty damn good start to the night.

Maybe we should talk about money a little bit. Money's an interesting thread to pull on, because it's something that I have always struggled with my whole life. There's almost a disdain for it at this point. Well, it's something that I've always wanted. I guess everybody wants money to some extent.

And when I was younger, I had dreams of being rich. I don't know — maybe that was inflated by my mom always telling me that I would be special and I would go on to do great things.

She used to tell me a story when I was little, about the first time I went to get a pair of shoes. She was poor, and I was probably a little bit older than I should have been to be just getting my first pair of shoes. But I was still little. She was trying on shoes, looking at them in the store. I think she said it was around Christmastime, although I might just be making those details up.

And she went to go put a box back on the shelf or something like that, and turned around, and there was this old lady. Very old. Just there in the aisle. And it startled her, because she didn't know how an old lady like that could just move so fast.

The old lady, I guess, started looking at me. And my mom was curious as to what was going on. And the old lady looked at her and she said, "Your son here is going to be something very special. He's a very special child."

And my mom kind of was like — haha — you know, nervously laughed it off, brushed it off. And she turned around for a moment to put back another box she was trying. And then when she turned around, the old lady was gone.

Now of course, my mom being who she was, attributed that to an angel telling her that her son would be special. She told me this story. And when I was younger, I just thought it was a story my mom told me. That maybe some crazy old lady said that to her and she was just attributing it as something supernatural.

It wasn't until later on in my life, when strange things like that started happening to me too, that I looked back on that story differently. I don't necessarily know if what my mom saw was 100% true. Looking at my own supernatural events, there's always a way to explain it. Make it logically fit with our rules of reality.

But that — plus the blue banner and my mom's constant praise — I think I started to build up this idea that I was special. That I was important somehow. That I would do something special.
