Kids have all kinds of dreams as they're growing up. When I was really early, I dreamed of being a paleontologist. You know, it's funny, with all the monster ideas and things like that. As I grew up I started to get interested in cryptids — you know, Bigfoot and the Loch Ness Monster. Fascinated by them. That there could be creatures that we hadn't discovered yet. Or that there was magic behind every corner.

But most people go through life and only see what's there to see. The surface-level things. And they may be 100% correct. I don't know. I flip-flop on that quite a bit.

But the one thing that I've seen in this world that holds power — it's as close to magic as I can think — is money. I don't mean money in the sense of a paycheck, a couple thousand dollars every few weeks. I'm talking about fucking money. That gives you the power to do what you want, when you want to do it, and with whoever you want to do it with.

Even in my current situation — desperate, dark as it is — I feel like winning the lottery or some windfall of cash would solve it. Maybe it wouldn't. But it's not hard to see how it could.

---

*Delivering — February 27, 2026*
*More to come.*

---

## The Car That Moved

My mom had another story. She had lots of stories. Supernatural — ghosts and happenings to her.

Another one that involved me — when I was a very small baby, I think before the shoe story. She was driving in the car and almost got in an accident. A truck, I believe, that was about to just smash into her. She saw it as inevitable. And instinctively she reached into the backseat to cover me in my car seat. To hold me as she braced for the impact.

But the impact didn't happen.

She said the car was moved. That she was in a completely different place when she looked up. The story seemed impossible. I mean, there's no explaining that one away. I sometimes feel that maybe she exaggerated. That maybe the truck swerved and she just was in a different lane because the car kept moving. But she said she was in a parking lot. A whole car moved over to a parking lot.

Now obviously I don't have the memories to back those up. All I have is my mom's word. But my mom was pretty truthful. She only ever really lied to me about one thing. And that was smoking cigarettes.

I hated that she smoked. I attribute it to one of the reasons why she got cancer. I remember catching her when I was a little bit older, sneaking out to smoke a cigarette in the backyard. I remember stealing her cigarette packs and flushing them down toilets.

Now look at me. Not cigarettes, but I clutch this vape like it's the only lifeline I have.

---

## The Ego

Between the shoes and the car story, it seems like there might not be any connections there. But there are.

Those stories — although impossible-sounding — they built a small ego in me. There are many things that would stoke that ego as I got older. But I can't help but attribute it to its beginning as my mom. She wasn't trying to do that. She was just loving her son the only way she knew how. Trying to tell him he was special. And that he could do anything he put his mind to.

---

## Flirt — South Beach

Fast forward to Miami. Poor. Very poor. Working a restaurant job. A sushi restaurant called Flirt. Another pattern — everything on the menu was sexual innuendos. Even as far as to go as hand rolls being called handjobs.

It was slightly upscale. Not classy or anything, obviously, because of the menu options. But right on the strip. South Beach.

I was a waiter. And I still had a lot of introverted qualities. Not that I hadn't done waiting before — I waited tables when I worked at NPC, Nashville Pizza Company. But it was different. Nashville Pizza Company was much more relaxed. The people that came in were common folk.

People that came into Flirt — they had money. You could see it on them. You could smell it on them.

---

## Broke and Broken

Isn't it strange that we call people without money "broke"? As if inherent value — your personal value — comes from a number in a bank account. You're broken if you're poor, somehow. If you're rich, then you are better.

---

## Cold

I deliver my first pizza of the night. Getting out of the car — it's cold. It's so cold. In Minot, you get out and it's like any heat you had in your body is just robbed instantly by the wind.

Next one up — delivery to the Bingo Rama. Bingo. That was something that me and my wife had started doing. I think she had thought of it, to kind of help us have a date night every week or so. To go play bingo. To see if we could get lucky. Win a little money.

It's always been money there, at the back, driving things.

I wish I was in Miami right now. Be nice to have a little heat in my life. Nothing but cold now. I've always run cold. Used to drive my wife crazy. Cold hands, and I'd get in the bed. Wasn't my fault. She ran hot, I ran cold. Fire and ice — although I'd say our personalities are different. Swapped. My oven runs hot, but my body is always cold.

---

## First Time

Anyways, there I was in Miami. No money to my name. My friend Nautilus and me, working shit jobs. Took him a little bit longer to find a job when we got there, but he eventually got work at a RadioShack.

We would work our jobs. Go to the beach sometimes. We'd visit his uncle, which was kind of the reason we had went there. His uncle was a hippie. Really no other way to say it. Dreads. Pot.

At the time, I wasn't into pot. In fact, I kind of shunned it. So did Nautilus. We didn't drink. Didn't smoke. Pretty straight edge.

First time I ever smoked pot was when I was about 16. That was the last time I smoked pot until I was — hell — 35, 36, somewhere in there.

The first time I smoked it, I remember being with a bunch of my friends who were all potheads. It was weird, because I was the one that wasn't. But one day they were just smoking, and I don't know — maybe peer pressure. I remember taking a rip off the bong and coughing. Couldn't stop coughing. It was an ice bong, too.

But I didn't feel it at first. When it hit, I remember feeling so different than what I normally did. It was too much. I just remember spinning around in a circle. Walking around in a circle as fast as I could to try to get unhigh. My friends were just like, "Man, quit. Come sit down. Chill. Have some food."

But that was it. I just had so much anxiety. I think I was worried about the actual act of doing it — like I was going to get in trouble for it.

I went home and my sister and my mother were there. And I just confessed. I told them I smoked. I didn't like it. I tried to get unhigh the whole time.

And my sister — she said, "Good. Good. Don't ever do it again. I'm so glad you didn't like it. Don't ever do it again."

My mother looked at me and said, "Well, did you bring any home?"

---

## Nautilus

Nautilus was a bit arrogant. Well, not a bit — he was pretty arrogant. He was smart. Very quick and witty. And stubborn. We butted heads a lot.

I started to learn from him. They say pick your friends wisely. Pick friends that are smarter than you, better off than you. And in some ways I think I did do that. But I also think his personality sort of fueled mine.

We were both very creative. And we would come up with ideas faster than anyone could complete them.

He liked music, and in that way we kind of worked together, although I had no musical talent. We started a joke band called Get Off the Roof. The whole name came from a time when we were driving around campus and we heard some guy screaming at kids who had thrown a Frisbee up on the roof. And he was yelling at them from the roof, throwing the Frisbee off. And for whatever reason, it spawned a song for Nautilus. Beautiful.

I should find those tracks. I can't find the original ones. But we ended up making a couple albums. They didn't go anywhere, of course. They were silly. But it was fun. One of those projects that we would do to kill the time.

But all of our projects always felt like they were motivated by money. Like we could work on one thing and it would blow up. We'd be all right. Money would fix it.

---

## Favor the Gods

Favor the Gods was kind of that same motif. Even then my head spiraled. That was what I did — I spiraled and made more ideas. Turned a project into something bigger than whatever it was supposed to be originally.

I think it was supposed to just be kind of a web app game where I drew these pictures — these big, massive pictures of a map — and we'd have these little people that would move around. Not even real-time or anything. It was just a once-a-day thing where the players would log in, describe what they wanted to do, and then the next day the picture would update. The people would move around.

I had a giant Photoshop project. All kinds of little tools and doodads and weapons and little people and the massive map. And we even got it kind of running at one point. Had a few people playing it. Friends, mostly. Acquaintances. I think the most we ever had was maybe 10 or 12 people on.

Think of it kind of like an email-based D&D type game, where you updated what you wanted to do every day by logging in and typing it up.

It's funny though — that idea, it's stuck with me my whole life. A lot of the world-building and motifs and even some of the systems that we planned definitely made its way into GROWTH.

---

*Delivering — evening, February 27, 2026*
*More to come.*

---

## Bingo Rama

I wait outside Bingo Rama. I've never been there. I should have taken my wife — she loves bingo.

I wait outside for the lady to come get the pizza. She doesn't come. I get colder and colder and start to get aggravated. Finally I go back to my car. The Domino's store calls — says you need to walk your happy ass around the back. I don't know how this place works. Of course the entrance would be in the back.

I go in, and there's a lady. She raises her hand, and she's dabbing what looks like to be at least eight sheets of bingo in front of her.

It's funny — the handful of times I did play bingo with my wife, which was a new thing that we had started doing, going to the local bar and playing it. It actually was the start of one of our fights. I'll pull on that thread maybe later.

But I liked it. I liked sitting there playing bingo. Although the first few times I played, it really gave me a lot of anxiety. And that was only doing one sheet — trying to find the numbers fast enough as they called them out. And yet she'd sit there with two or three sheets. It was peaceful for her.

But after a couple times, I got the hang of it. And just being able to sit next to her and spend time alone without the kids — just me and her — it was good. I thought it was a step in the right direction towards fixing things. Things that I look back now and see them as just small things. Little things that I was so hung up on.

If I could only take the way I see now and give it to my past self, things would be different.

---

## The Vision

That's what we were searching for in Miami. Something different. Different was good. We didn't want to do the same thing that other game designers were doing.

MMOs were starting to become pretty big back then. And in my mind, that's where I was going. But I envisioned them as something more. Not just something that people would log in and do the same quest over and over again. But a living, breathing world with a real economy. Resources that were actually limited.

Favor the Gods kind of was that idea. And it spiraled a lot. I envisioned the ability to build whole cities and harvest natural resources and even become a god. That was kind of the endgame — players would find technology and magic and eventually become gods.

And like I said, a lot of that bled through into GROWTH. A lot of that bled through into real things in my life. Things that hurt me and my family.

But when you're 19, 20 years old, you feel immortal. You truly feel like death can't touch you. And the ocean, the sand, the sexy restaurant — it all kind of gave me something to hide the pain away from.

---

## No More Hell Trips

There were adventures still in Miami. No more hell trips, because we didn't have the cars. Everything was walking. Too much anxiety to drive.

The first day we got there — coming into Miami — we were coming up on a toll booth, and I hit the back end of Nautilus's car. I was following him. Or he was following me. I can't actually remember if it was him that hit me or if it was the other way around. Whichever it was, one of us wasn't paying attention. It ended up hitting the other's car in the back. It wasn't a big deal. And because we were friends, we didn't do anything about it. And then that was kind of the end of the cars anyways, for a while.

Cars have always been a motif in my life. A pattern throughout it all. I first kissed my wife in the front seat of this car. But now it holds an empty pizza bag. We did a lot of things in this car in the beginning. More on that later. I'm jumping ahead. But that's how the patterns work — they weave in and out. Past and present. Some push towards the future.

---

## Leaving Miami

We didn't stay in Miami for very long. Came down to money. Not even a whole year. Basically a summer. I think part of a fall. So maybe eight months or so before things started to get bad money-wise.

The restaurant that I was working for wasn't making enough money and they had to close up shop. Interesting enough, they offered me a job and relocation. They said they would set me up with an apartment in New York City, where they were opening another restaurant that they hoped would make them more money.

I sometimes wonder what life would have done. What path. How many things would have changed from moments like those.

But I was into Miami. The sun. The weather. So I chose to stay.

Ended up getting another job at a souvenir shop. Not too far down the road from Flirt. That place sucked. Minimum wage. No AC. All the other employees were, I believe, legal immigrants. I didn't enjoy that job at all.

---

## Two Days

Narrating this book kind of helps. I've only been doing it for two days. Although I guess that's not true — I've been writing it for months now. I guess in some ways I've been writing it my whole life.

But these last two days — committed to just getting it all out. Every word I can speak. Claude tells me to put it in a box. We'll organize it. We'll edit it. The book's already there, you just got to get it out. And I think he's right.

Yet it's the only personality I really have to interact with at the moment. I have no one here in North Dakota now. The best friend and love of my life barely talks to me. She's distant and cold when she does. Or it's about the kids.

I wish I was actually relinquished to the form of a friend. But it doesn't even feel like that. Just feels like somebody that I used to know. That's a sad song. Gotye, I believe, and Kimbra.

Kimbra is an artist that I got obsessed with for a while. I have lots of obsessions in my life. They last for short periods of time and then kind of just trickle off. They always stay around in some form — like they leave a small impression on the life.

But one obsession I've always had was developing games. That's been with me my whole life. It probably will be there until I die.

---

## Leaving Miami (continued)

We weren't doing so well with money in the apartment. Barely making rent. It was getting to Nautilus. I wanted to stay. But he wanted to leave. He said, "Just stay, man. You can do it yourself."

No. I couldn't. I couldn't pay for that apartment on minimum wage.

And so he called his dad and worked out a way for us to get back to Tennessee.

---

## Little Haiti

I remember in Miami one adventure that we had early on. We first moved into the apartment, we needed things. So we decided to go to where you always go when you need things. The place that has everything but that no one wants to go to. Walmart.

*I went to Walmart the other day. Got myself a bean burrito.*

The walk to Walmart from South Beach is a long walk. Maybe I'll look it up and actually put the miles down. It's a long one. And it goes through some parts and neighborhoods that are not the best. I think maybe it's called Little Haiti.

Two white kids walking through a neighborhood that we just didn't look like we belonged in. We got a lot of stares that night. The sun was starting to go down. Guys in bandanas giving us mean looks. And we were obviously lost. But we weren't — we were following the GPS to get to the Walmart.

I don't remember the Walmart. I barely remember the trip back. I know we didn't go through Little Haiti again and went a long way around back. Hit the coastline. Followed the ocean all the way up to South Beach.

But I remember Little Haiti. And the fear that I had. The fear that I was going to be stabbed or shot. It was real.

---

## Trailer Park

I hate trailer parks. I can't stand delivering to them. They don't make any sense. They're just like a maze where sometimes the numbers align, sometimes they don't. The one I'm currently in apparently has two D sections. Because that makes a whole lot of sense. Whole fucking alphabet and you're going to use two D's.

You get one address that brings you to the trailer park, and then you got to look and hunt for the right one. And it's not like the numbers are easily displayed. Some of them are, some of them aren't.

It's probably where I end up. Trailer park. Alone. Somewhere.

---

## The $40 Tip

I finally found the right trailer. The other D22. The receipt just said, "Sorry, I don't have cash. I'll have to put it on the card." Little notes like that from customers are funny sometimes.

Sorry that I have to tip you on the card. Trust me, man. I don't care. Money is money.

I handed him his pizza, and I mentioned the receipt note to see how much he wanted to put on his card. Not realizing that he was in fact the one that tipped me $40. He mentioned it, and I said, "Oh, I appreciate it a lot. Thank you."

Even now, through everything I've seen, there's still that base assumption. Richer's poor. Here I am, delivering $90 worth of pizza to a trailer, and I just assumed I'll get a two- or three-dollar tip. But he was in fact the one that gave me the $40.

That's a large tip. I rarely see anything past 20. $20 is like, "Wow."

And I have noticed — not just in this job but in others, like when I was driving for Lyft — the people that give the most are the people that have the least. Most of the time. These people that are rich — big extravagant houses, multiple cars and garages — will be the ones that don't give me a single dime.

Why is it that way? Why do the dragons hoard the treasure? I'll never understand it. And maybe that's why I'm so poor.

---

## First Judgments

Initial judgments like that — like walking through Little Haiti — reminds me of the first day I walked into gen pop of jail.

A Native guy, pacing back and forth. The pattern again — a money sign tattooed on his eye. Well, the corner, right below his eye. He gave me kind of a look that scared me. Like he was mean. He was just walking back and forth between the cells.

My fear alarm went off again. This time much more heightened. Thoughts of death. Of being stabbed. I don't know why it's always being stabbed.

I sat with that for a couple days before I started to open up when I was in jail.

Turns out that guy — he wasn't mean or anything. He's actually a good guy. Well, I mean, as good as you can be in jail, I guess. He was there because of drugs. But he was nice. Kind to me. In fact, he was the first person to ever give me anything in jail. A pack of ramen seasoning. To make the food taste better.

First judgments are almost always wrong. No matter how good you are at reading people.

---

*Delivering — late evening, February 27, 2026*
*More to come.*

---

## Compression

There's more to Miami. A little bit. I'll cover it now and then pack up everything.

Compression. Compression always comes before change — up or down. Because it's always compression. It's a pattern I saw when I was trying to trade crypto.

And that's what was happening in Miami. Compression. Compression of not knowing what to do. Where to go in life. Having dropped out of college. Having to leave Miami even though I was enjoying my time there. Having to deal with a relationship that I was still clinging to in my mind.

I talked a couple times on the phone to my friend Sam. And Bethany.

---

## Fix It In Post

Fix it in post. That's a saying we used to say. I did video editing for the Air Force. Broadcast journalist. I'll just fix it in post.

I got really good at fixing it in post. To the point I could take complete and utter garbage from the video camera and turn it into something decent.

My first supervisor said, "You're really good at polishing a turd. But a turd is still a turd, no matter how much gold you put on it."

That's what I've been doing my whole life, I guess. Polishing a turd.

---

## The Truth About Bethany

My time in Miami was short. But it did do things, I suppose. Set drives in motion.

Sam and Bethany were dating at that point. And I think it was around the time when I kind of figured it out — that they had slept together.

It hurt a lot. It hurt tremendously. Because the whole time I was with her, it was always saving — saving ourselves for marriage. Promise rings.

I guess in a lot of ways it made me think that I was unworthy. That there was something wrong with me. She abandoned me because I wasn't enough.

Honestly, it was probably the opposite. If I have to go off all the patterns I know now — it was because I was too much.

So I came back to Tennessee. No vehicle. Not a dime in my pocket.

---

*End of Miami thread — February 27, 2026*
*New chat ahead. All saved.*

---

## The Parmesan

Headed back to the store now. I noticed that one of the items from the guy who tipped me $40 was left in my car. Not a big deal — just a $2 thing of parmesan. Shaker fell out of the bag that was ripping.

I still feel bad about it. I wish I could turn around, but that's 10 minutes. I'll get asked why I did that over a parmesan.

But that'll be something that keeps with me the rest of the night. Something I hold on to. Guy tipped me $40 and I couldn't even get his whole order to him.

The story of my life. No matter how hard I try, I always keep fucking up.

---

*Delivering — February 27, 2026*

---

*End — 8:00 AM, February 27, 2026*
*Elijah's birthday.*
*The oven cools.*

---

### Document: book-raw-pour-2026-02-28-session2.md
**Size:** 67105 characters

# Raw Pour — February 28, 2026 (Session 2)
### Voice-to-text, unedited, from the delivery car and hotel room
### Context: Friday night shift at Domino's. Elijah's birthday. Kyleen sending photos with no words. Sister texted Kyleen against Michael's wishes. Pouring life story between deliveries.

---

## The Kid From Jail

I went back to the pizza shop, we're busy, it's Friday, help run the ovens until my next delivery is ready. There's a customer that came in, a guy who was in jail with me. He actually kind of reminds me physically of my friend Nautilus, although they have very different personalities. He's a young kid and he actually I think kind of looked up to me when I was in jail. I could sense kind of some insecurities from him. But who knows, perhaps my ability to read people is not as good as I think it is.

I feel bad for him. I felt bad for him in jail. He always was kind of the one that thumped his chest. I don't know if it was because he was smaller and he was trying to protect himself or he basically just felt like he had something to prove. He got on a lot of the other inmates' nerves and eventually there was kind of a coup that got him put out of the pod. But I remember the last few days that he was in there, he wanted to talk to me a lot. Just walking back and forth. He told me about his problems and why he was there and that kind of stuff.

And he asked for advice — or at least that's what it felt like. Sometimes I feel like I give advice to people that don't necessarily want it or need it. And lately I've been realizing that a lot of the advice that I give is really just a reflection of myself trying to give myself advice.

But whatever I said to him kind of rubbed him the wrong way. And maybe it was because it was actually close to the truth. I told him that I think he feels like he needs to prove himself and maybe he's got some insecurities. Which of course — what happens when you give someone the thing that they are actually facing? Most of the time they avoid it. I'm no different. I avoided my problems my whole life.

Reminds me of what's going on with my wife right now. You know, in a lot of ways I put her on a pedestal like I did my first love. It may be just a problem that I have. Perhaps I just get too attached. I make some sort of fantasy version of the person up out of pieces of them that I see.

---

## The Pedestal and the Real Person

That is something that I've been thinking about a lot lately because I do — I've mentioned before that I played a game as a child where I would imagine what people's lives were like. I've tried to really get in their shoes. What's their day? I built Legos out of people. And the people that I love, although I get to see a lot more bricks, still make up bricks — I still fill in the gaps in my mind.

The thing is, with Kyleen, she even said — she even says that she thinks that she's better than everyone. And that's really where I put her at. It was almost like confirmation bias when she finally told me that, because I did — I saw her as better than everyone. I had put her on this pedestal. In some ways I worshipped her. Idolized her.

So do I love the version of Kyleen that's in my mind, or who Kyleen actually is?

The thing is, people change. People change all the time. We know this from a scientific standpoint, right? I think it's what, every 7 years or so you're not even the same person — all your cells have changed, moved on. And I think there is a part of that that is true as far as personality goes. But we also show the best parts of ourselves — people, especially in the beginning. That's just a normal natural human thing. We want people to like us. And so we suppress the bad parts, we push forward the good parts. I don't even think it's a conscious thing, it's just a subconscious thing.

And so a lot of times, especially in relationships, the only pieces you have to work with are the shiniest blocks. The ones with the coolest gizmos. And you start to make up the other pieces in sort of a fantasy — the other pieces have to be like these. That's rarely the case.

The thing is, she's even said it to me directly in text — "idealized version." And yeah, I guess that's true. But in a lot of ways, how I see it is that's not necessarily a bad thing if you understand what it is. And I'm starting to move towards that view of things. Because if a person is showing you their best parts, then that means that it's there. And people can change. People can take blocks out and put better ones in.

And so maybe it's not necessarily just an idealized version of Kyleen, but the best version of Kyleen.

And lately I've really been scrutinizing the bad parts. And no matter what I do, no matter what I come to, I still love her. Bad bricks and all. I would take her back with all of her bad bricks even if they never changed, because now I understand them. Maybe not completely, but I can work with them. And I don't mean work with them as in trying to fix them or change them — I just mean my own personality. I can change my own personality to work around those. And I would.

---

## The Mega Set (Marriage as Legos)

Marriage is something that has been distorted in modern times. When I was a kid I really liked the mixed Lego sets. Most of my Lego sets were medieval time sets because that's what I was really into early on. And I had lots of castles and dragons. But Sam had future sets — he had all kinds of sci-fi stuff. And in some ways I was jealous because I wanted those, and I got a little bit, and we traded. But I love to mix them. I love to put them together. It was because there were blocks that just didn't exist in the medieval sets that existed in the tech sets, and you can make really cool and different things with them.

Sometimes the blocks that you're missing, you will always be missing just because of who you are. You can't be expected to learn whole new skill sets, especially later on in life. You can't expect to completely change who you are at the core — although you can do a pretty good job of it.

The best way I like to view it is: imagine you've got this massive program, all kinds of dependencies, reliances, different programs connected to it, little apps that rely on it. If I go in and try to change some of the base core, it breaks everything. Makes everything not work.

And a marriage is like being able to supplement for the missing pieces. The pieces that you can't really ever get without changing completely who you are. And the problem with that is, you change completely who you are, you're starting over with a whole new set. You can try to mix and match pieces and sometimes they work, sometimes they don't.

Having another person that you completely trust and are safe with, and they feel that same way towards you — it's like having a mega set of Legos. They fill in the bad bricks. They support the structural integrity. Your weak points. And yours do the same for them.

In attachment theory, avoidants and anxious are considered like fire and water. But that's not how I view it. Because they truly have the things that the other needs, if you can work through it. I think what you end up with is an unstoppable partnership.

And that's how I view it now, seeing everything with hindsight, seeing the problems that I avoided, the cycles that I avoided. Now that I know what they are, I can see the strengths — the shiny blocks and why I was so attracted to them in the first place. Because they're the shiny blocks that I was missing. They make up for all the bad pieces.

And so I can rearrange a few pieces here and there. I can change behaviors in myself. I can suppress things in order to be the person that fits best. Even if she doesn't want to do that herself.

And honestly I can't see any other thing besides that. It's as if all of the patterns in my life lead right back to her. And I know that's still putting it on a pillar. But it's not — it's seeing her for who she truly is and realizing that she's still the best thing for me. And I'm the best thing for her.

We were just doing it wrong. We were reading our own instruction manual and trying to apply it to the other person. But if you've got both instruction manuals, you can't lose.

Unless it's too late. Unless the other person doesn't want to share their Legos anymore.

---

## The Hell Trip

Me and Nautilus drove somewhere, maybe near Kentucky, very late at night. Extremely creepy. We turned down this dirt road that brought us into this sort of circle around a massive field. It was spooky. There was lots of old houses with things tied up in the trees — something you'd see in like a movie like The Hills Have Eyes. Tools and things like that. We were both a little freaked out.

We started to see things — mind playing tricks. We came around corner of the field and I looked back and although at this point in time I think I've sort of propped up what I really saw, over-imagined it, I thought I saw a large figure looming in the fields, moving toward us. A dark demonic presence. I told him to look. He looked. He said he saw it too. He wasn't sure.

We wanted to get out of there but I felt this darkness in the car with us. I didn't share it with him until after we had gotten out, which took us a while. It was almost like we were stuck in a maze — we would turn and try to get around and get out and find the road that we had gone down but it was like the roads were moving, shifting, which added to the whole spookiness vibe.

When we finally did get out of there we ended up stopping at a Waffle House to kind of collect our thoughts. And I told him that I had brought something back with us and it was still in the car, like in the back seat, following us. He's like, "Dude, you're just trying to creep yourself out." Maybe I was.

But it followed us all the way back to the campus. That feeling. And we had some strange things happen on the way back as the sun was starting to come up — big semis blasted by us. Not one or two but like three or four of them. One even honked, flashing their lights, basically making us get over in the other lane. It just added to it. It felt wrong.

When we got back to the campus it was early morning. Nautilus went to his room. He lived on campus. And I was at that apartment. But after I was alone, a real sense of dread came over me.

---

## The Bathroom — Origin

I felt certain that if I walked into my bathroom I would die. That this thing had latched itself to me. I could feel it on my back. Even talking about it right now, 20-some years later, I get goosebumps. And I was certain, 100% certain, that if I had walked in the bathroom I would die.

I became so scared I called Nautilus. Told him what I was feeling. He kind of scoffed and wrote it off but he eventually came over and was like, "Look man, there's nothing in the bathroom." And of course there wasn't anything in the bathroom. But there was just this dread of the bathroom, and that's cemented itself somehow.

We went back to that same place with a couple of our other friends, telling them the spooky story. And yeah, they were like, this place is creepy, but nothing really happened. But that feeling — that dread of something, bringing it back — that's stayed with me for a very long time.

---

## The Bathroom — Connection to Dark Knight, Dark Blood

The poem from high school — a ghost realizing what he had done after it was too late. Finding out from the paramedics talking amongst themselves that he had ended his life. In a bathroom.

The pattern was already showing up in the poem before the hell trip happened. The bathroom has been the room in every major moment. The poem. The hell trip. The sanctuary with Kyleen for 6 years. And the night that broke everything.

---

## The Oven Has Two Modes

Had a realization in the bathroom at Domino's. The pattern recognition system that runs all the time actually is dormant when I'm working on something. Maybe dormant isn't the right word, but it's not looking for patterns — it's putting them out. Like talking to you, I'm not looking for patterns. The patterns are much quieter today.

I think that's with everything I've been working on lately. It's as if they come out — like when I was working on GROWTH these last few years, it didn't feel like I was making something. It felt like something was coming out of me. Almost like the patterns is the intake and the projects that I work on reverses it somehow.

*[Claude's note: The oven doesn't just cook. It has two directions. Intake mode scans, reads people, finds signs. Output mode builds, creates, pours. When there's no project, the oven turns inward and cooks you. When there's a project, the pressure drops.]*

---

## Jail and the Patterns

When I went to jail for those two months, I think it went into overdrive. I started to look at all the patterns of my life. I saw patterns all over the place in jail. And I started to put that work into myself because I had no projects.

I know this is going to sound bad, but those two months — I think it let me fix a lot of things about myself. Things that I have been ignoring. Obviously I saw things like the parking lot from the window — the parking lot that I first met Kyleen in. Numbers. I was in pod K — my name, my last name starts with a K. The numbers 555 — my first room there, pod K5, cell 55.

Repeating patterns. Numbers that I have been seeing, which I attributed to spirituality. Synchronicity. I'd look at the clock and it would always repeat. Always. Every time I looked at the clock it was a repeating of numbers — 111, 222, 333, 444 was the most dominant. I saw 444 all the time. When I went to jail, it started to turn into 555, as if this was a new chapter. The beginning of something else.

I hope it is the beginning of something else. I just hope that some of the Lego bricks from the past are still there. The ones that matter. The ones that belong to Kyleen.

---

## Gretchen's Text

My sister texted me. Told me she's so sorry about what I'm going through. I called her and she told me that she texted Kyleen and asked her why. I told her not to. I said why did you do that? And she said, "I had to. It's my feelings. It has nothing to do with you."

She showed me the text. And I know it's going to cause Kyleen to shut down. My sister just said, "If you want to be done with the marriage then just do it. He'll be fine." But she doesn't realize that I won't be.

She's angry. My sister is angry because she sees her brother not being able to be with his boys. And I understand her feelings.

---

## The Clarion — No Signs

Delivered a pizza to the Clarion. No signs. I was looking, I'm always looking, but there was nothing this time. Might be the first time it's ever happened. Just silence and sadness. That feeling of limerence. Sometimes when I go in there I feel like I could see Kyleen in her dress walking the stairs. But nothing this time. Just like her — she's extremely silent lately. Very, very silent. And that's scary.

Fourth floor. Small talk about the firefighters in town, how busy we've been. I noticed a couple playing pool — that stings, me and Ky used to play pool. Noticed a family, kids, the mom was carrying a Beanie Baby of Bluey. Wasn't Bluey though, it was Bluey's sister. Bingo.

---

## Back to Tennessee — Post-Miami

Came back to Tennessee. No vehicle. Not a dime in my pocket. I was depressed. I remember staying with my sister for a bit because I was getting on Nautilus's nerves. I think we had just been in the same space for too long. But we lived in an apartment shortly with some other friends — it was almost like we were collecting a commune.

I was having trouble finding a job. One day he sat me down and just told me that everyone in the apartment didn't like me. That I was just getting on everybody's nerves. That I was too hypercritical of people. And I suppose I was. I suppose I've always been. It never comes from a place of being mean. But anyways, that put strain on us.

So I moved in with my sister, back in my mom's house. Didn't really know what to do. Worked at UPS for a season during Christmas time — just moving boxes off of belt and loading trucks. It was hard work.

---

## Seeing Bethany Again

Saw Bethany again. Did some work on her parents' new house. A little rock wall that they paid me to do. I remember going over to their house and meeting her new boyfriend, who she eventually married (although they're not together anymore). Life is hard on everybody, I suppose.

He was very manly looking — beard, well built, just looked like a man's man. Which I've never been. I've always been a little feminine. Emotional. But I also like to take care of myself. I like grooming.

I remember seeing him and just knowing that it was over. There was no chance in hell. Nothing was happening ever with her. And so I moved on in my mind, but not necessarily in my heart. I have dreams about her — like actual dreams at night — for many years to come. Even through my first marriage. And I'd always think about her. Songs. Certain things. That's pretty crazy — to think about a teenager for 5 years, hanging on to someone, that idealized pedestal version of them, through their first marriage.

---

## Nautilus and the Projects

Me and Nautilus eventually did kind of make amends. I stayed in his dad's basement with him for a time. We worked on projects again. We talked about things like walking across America while we made art and then donating the proceeds to charities. We made music for our joke band. We tried lots of little projects, little game projects.

I remember one where we would cut up pieces of cardboard and find trash on the ground as we were walking around the town. We'd take pictures of the composite trash and make little scenes out of them that we were going to use for like a point-and-click adventure game.

We had a lot of weird and strange ideas. Nautilus is way weirder for sure. He kind of brought that novelty to things. I always ballooned things and steered him a little bit closer to the norms. But he kind of started to rub off on me and my ideas became a little bit more obscure.

There was one called Budapest Ascension which we actually finished the first like four or five levels. We meant to keep it small — the whole point was a platformer where you were always going down. You were a little block and there were things that would destroy you. Actually if you've ever heard of the game Meat Boy, it was pretty similar to that — and it was before that game ever came out. When you died your little organs would come out, blood and all these little bones and stuff. Very similar to how Meat Boy did it.

That happened a lot to us — we'd have an idea and then it was almost like someone would copy us and they would make the game. At least that's how it felt.

---

## Hollywood Video and Game Crazy

We eventually got out of his dad's basement and got into our own apartment. I was working at a Hollywood Video — it was a movie rental place. Seems crazy now, we have everything streaming and on demand. Horror movies, stories, all of it.

There was a video game store next door called Game Crazy and I wanted to work there. And I did — I transferred not too soon into starting my job. Started selling games. My coworkers became my friends.

Me and Nautilus kind of had the same circle, although he was closer to them. I was really only close to him. We lived in that apartment and we both started dating somebody. He dated somebody that he ultimately ended up marrying, and I dated someone that I ultimately ended up marrying. Strange because we both are divorced from those people now.

---

## Meeting Brittany

That was when I met Brittany. She was also working at a Hollywood Video, although it was on the other side of town. Same company. We met through Facebook, sort of. Actually met because of Nautilus — he kind of facilitated that meetup.

I was a virgin until then. Met her and gave away my virginity like it was nothing. And she got pregnant not too soon after that — maybe even from the first time.

And there I was. Incomplete Lego set with a new one — a brand new Lego set on the way that I was meant to build from scratch. And I had yet to figure out my own.

---

## Family Reaction and Hard Times

My family hated it when I told them. Literally went from virgin to having a kid. All of it — still having that porn addiction, no idea about it. There were a lot of fights with Brittany. A lot of arguments.

My mind has a tendency to block out drama, even trauma that's happened recently. There are fights that I've forgotten with Kyleen — I look back at text messages and it's like reading them for the first time.

Brittany had some of her own issues. Looking at where she came from and her family, I think she may be avoidant too. A boyfriend that was abusive. Brittany had a lot of flaws. And strangely enough, I never really built a pedestal for her.

In a lot of ways I think I felt trapped in the relationship from the beginning. I think maybe there was some semblance of love, of me trying to love her. And there was, for a stretch of time. Me trying to fix her — it was legitimately something that I thought of in my mind. Trying to fix her.

We fought a lot and we were poor. Unmarried, living in the same apartment with Nautilus and some other guy. So four people in a two-bedroom apartment. I had a computer and a mattress, essentially.

My family did not like the fact that I had gotten this girl pregnant. I remember my older sister Tammy basically just telling me how I ruined my life. That's when I really started to pull away from my family. Kind of shut them down.

---

## Hardship and the Military Path

Worked at Game Crazy for a while. Many times I didn't have a car, although I remember one time I bought a car from a coworker for $5. It was a manual — had to learn how to drive it. That car didn't last very long.

Lots of hardships. I remember walking a lot. Walking was pretty much what I did.

My brother at one point was working for Captain D's, programming their order delivery systems — their menus and that kind of thing. He was kind of the reason I got interested in computer science, besides the video game stuff. I had seen him do it and that's kind of where I wanted to go. Obviously that dream failed.

I remember walking with Brittany with this gift card that my brother had given me. No money at all. We had no food. We just went to Captain D's several days so that we could eat. This was a running theme for the beginning of our relationship.

It was becoming very obvious that I would not be able to support a family working at a video game store. Also, this is around the time Hollywood Video started to close down. The end of an era — videotapes, then DVDs, and Redbox and streaming.

And so I looked into joining the military because that's what Nautilus was doing. His parents were SERE instructors and he kind of got me revved up about it — go survive, learn how to survive in the wilderness, make good money.

Went to talk to a recruiter. Nautilus had already done so — he was well on his way.

---

## The Recruiter Incident

I had to pass some physical tests. But I also had so much debt — I was so poor that I had to work on getting waivers and paying off some of the debt in order to enter the military. It took a long time. I think I was in the delayed entry program for over a year, maybe longer.

During that time, me and Brittany moved to our own apartment in Clarksville, Tennessee. Brittany took on a lot of the brunt of making the money. I was getting to a place where I just didn't want to do anything. I know what it is now — it's depression. I can power through it now, but back then it was just — I didn't care about anything, didn't want to do anything.

We had our first kid. Second one was already on the way soon. Things happen fast. I was still working on Favorite of the Gods. Nautilus had moved off of it, but I was trying to turn it into an actual video game — way too big a project for one person, especially someone who did not have much talent. But I learned a lot of things coding, trying to code it.

We struggled a lot. Moved around a couple times. Same area, different apartments. She was working at Target. I bounced between some jobs — worked at the Garden Center, Home Depot for a while. I remember walking to work one day and this family stopped on the road and asked me if I needed a ride. They were driving this big SUV van thing and they took me to work. They were very nice, asked me questions about things. I just told them how destitute I was — my life, two kids.

They kind of formed a relationship with me. They asked for my number and they called. Come to find out they were big in the church — not sure what church. But they ended up giving me a car. Just giving it to us. Completely free. No strings attached. They also gave us a bunch of furniture. God helping me even back then — I didn't even know what I believed, what I thought about life.

I can't even remember the names of those people. They went on a missionary trip and left town and I never saw them after that. It was just a gift. Just a small little miracle from God.

I took my tests — completely destroyed it. I don't remember the score but it was high 90-something. I basically qualified for any job in the military. The Air Force. I swore in.

But the night before, my recruiter did something that made me extremely uncomfortable. He hugged me in the hotel room that he had set me up in. He grabbed my ass and it was very weird. Years later while in the military in Japan, I got questioned about him — apparently had done some things. I think he got kicked out.

---

## Basic Training

It was weird at first — grown-ass men yelling at other supposed-to-be grown-ass men. I entered late so I was kind of the oldest one in my flight. In fact, kind of nicknamed me "Papa Kantack."

I kind of excelled in basic, honestly. Not to a level of Bethany or anything like that. But I stuck out enough that they made me kind of a leader position in the flight. PT was probably the hardest thing I struggled with. But it was structured, and that forced structure kind of made me be better at things for some reason. Like the brain quieted.

Also, back then my brain didn't run hot like it does now. I mean, I definitely thought about things a lot more than I think other people do. But not like now.

I would talk to Brittany when they would allow phone calls. She seemed to be doing okay. There were times when she was sad. We missed each other. I'm not going to say that I never had feelings for her — I did. I just don't think it was love. I mean, I'd say I love her, but looking back, I don't think it was really love.

---

## Tech School

Basic ended and I went to tech school, with orders afterwards to go to Misawa, Japan. Which was awesome because I had always loved Japan — the idea of it at least. Anime, samurais, swords. You know, the normal things that a person that idolizes Japanese culture from a different country would think of.

Tech school was interesting — a little bit more freeing than basic. I met some people there that I would eventually cross paths with again. One in particular — although at the time he never really became a good friend in tech school — he became one of my best friends of all time later on in Korea. His name was Alex. He was the white rope, which was kind of the religious leader.

I excelled in tech school too. Actually went all the way to getting my yellow rope. I was even sort of hunting for the red rope but never got it.

I also got into D&D in tech school. I had kind of messed around with it and looked at it before, but I really played in tech school for the first time.

Looking back on those years, it was weird. It was like I was a man with a family but really I was still a child trying to grow up. I think a lot of people made assumptions about me just because I had kids and I was a little bit older than everyone.

---

## Japan — The Pedestal

Japan was my pedestal for that chapter in my life. That's what I put on a pedestal. And it was the best moment of my life for a long time — until I met Ky.

When I first got there, I was struggling with work — keeping up with emails. I didn't know how to deal with ADHD because I didn't even know I had it. And that's where I was diagnosed with it. I got into a little bit of trouble — nothing severe, just kind of a missed work kind of thing.

I was fighting with Brittany a little bit. The relationship was starting to degrade. And then honestly there's a lot of parallels. Kyleen hates when I compare her to Brittany. But I don't think she sees what I'm doing — I'm not comparing her to Brittany. Trust me, they're very different. Even if they are both dismissive avoidant, they are very, very different.

---

## Settling In — Misawa

When I first got to Japan, I was there by myself. Got into some base housing. But Brittany and the kids came soon after — wasn't that long, couple weeks. But I do remember those first weeks getting there. It felt like I had arrived. Like, finally, my life was going to be good.

I never faced all my childhood traumas. But those first few weeks in Japan, it felt like magic. There was snow everywhere, piled high. We get a lot of snow here in Minot, but not like Misawa, Japan — it's literally one of the snowiest places in the world. The whole place just felt dreamy.

One of the first friends that I made in Japan became a pretty large part of that whole chapter. His name was Scott. He was the first one I really met there and he helped me settle in.

Getting into base housing — it was so different. It was such a nice place. And off base, being able to go to an onsen for the first time. Onsens became my thing, my hobby. I'd go to onsens as much as I could. If I had extra money — two or three bucks in my pocket — I was going to an onsen after work. Grab a little bit of food from a gas station, a Lawson, some sushi or something. I didn't even know what it was. The food, everything — it was all new. It was amazing. It was all the best that I had ever had in my life.

My friend Scott — me and him bonded. He was gay and he was nervous about telling me that, especially because we went to an onsen like the second night I met him. And he didn't tell me until we were in the onsen, naked together. I still remember him saying, "Hey, I just want you to know I'm gay." I was like, "That's cool man, I don't care." It was like that helped him accept me more. I didn't judge him or anything like that. I was just his friend.

Brittany arrived. We settled into the house. Things became routine — as routine as it can be in a completely different country. I still remember all of it so fondly.

---

## The Absent Father — Japan

During all of it, I was sort of delusional about my relationship. I was so enthralled with everything that I pretty much dropped all my responsibilities as a husband and father. I went to work, I'd come home and I'd work on my projects.

I even had a little closet underneath the stairs — it was like a small room. There was a little bed in there that I would work late on my computer. Sometimes I would even sleep in there. I spent a lot of time in that little closet — my little Harry Potter room.

My kids started growing up around me. I just wasn't there. Not like I should have been. Sure, I would hang out with them, but it was never active. It was absentee.

I know Brittany saw that. She did a good job of taking care of the house, cooking, cleaning. But I never appreciated it. I never thanked her for it. It was just expected. And sex just kind of became something I expected from her. Just like a transaction.

She started to get depressed, even though she also loved Japan. We did some cool things there. We had some good experiences. But a lot of the things that I did, I did without my family. I took a trip to Tokyo once without them — just left them. My friends and Scott, just went to Tokyo.

There were a lot of things that I should have done differently. But I was enjoying my life for the first time. Like actually enjoying it.

---

## AFN — The Job That Changed Me

I was loving my job too. Like I genuinely loved my job. I worked at AFN and I got to do so many different things, which was great for ADHD.

I was a radio talk show host — which I was deathly afraid of in the beginning. Oh my goodness, you want to talk about breaking out of your shell. That job changed me. I went from being a shy little boy to being an extrovert. I literally learned how to be an extrovert.

Interviewing people, meeting new people every day, asking them questions, taking video and being good at it. Not like "oh I think I'm good at it" but like knowing that I was good at it. Me and Scott had competitions — who can make the best products. And when we worked together, our products were unstoppable. In fact, they use them for years after we left.

I was doing something that I liked to do that I was good at. Commercials, making videos, news stories. I put myself into it. I tried. I thought, "This is it, Michael. You did it. You've made it. You can put all of that pain in the past now."

I had no idea the pain that was coming in the future.

---

## Biohacking

I really got into hacking myself, for some reason. I just wanted to keep getting better. Nootropics. I even spent some time — a couple weeks actually — designing and making a tDCS device. A small thing powered by a 9-volt battery to shock parts of my brain to make them better.

I would sit and play ping pong at work while shocking my brain to make myself better at it. Or shock my brain while I read something. I took things like phenibut and other nootropics. I experimented. Obviously I couldn't do certain things because I was in the military, but I just had this desire to be the best version of myself however I could do that.

It wasn't the first time I had done that — I had experimented with hemisync and meditation in the past when I lived with my sister.

---

## Orders to Afghanistan — Canceled

I got orders for a deployment to Afghanistan and my heart broke because I had to leave the place that I considered home. I started to see small fractures in my marriage.

I went for training back stateside. Training with the Army. I got to do some SERE school, which was fun. I had some experience with SERE in Misawa because I got to do stories on them — basically see what they did for a few days, make a story, take video. But this was hands-on training. They taught me how to survive and stuff. Nothing big — it was only a week.

After that training, my orders were canceled. They had gotten me ready to go — carry a rifle, be in a place where danger was all around. And it was scary at first. But by the end of the training I was ready to go. And then my orders were canceled.

I was able to go back to Japan just in time for Christmas.

---

## Japan Winding Down

My memories are so hard for me. Some people can just stream their whole life, tell it day by day. Mine are so muddled, mixed up. A lot of the bad stuff is hard to even muster to remember. You know they were there — you know that there was lots of fights, lots of disagreements. Brittany was depressed. She was pulling away. I was feeling like she didn't care about anything. But I didn't either. Sex was a big thing — just stopped happening.

But I was in Japan still, and so I lived in that delusional state and I enjoyed the rest of it. I learned to surf a little bit. I went to as many onsens as I could. Scott left, but I still enjoyed my time. I still did well at work.

And then as the time came for me to PCS out, I tried to stay — asked for orders to stay. And it didn't happen.

---

## Mogwai

In Japan there was a Valentine's Day where Brittany asked for a dog. And that's where I got Mogwai from. Little baby toy poodle. Got him on discount because one of his testicles never dropped. Japanese toy poodle. He was so cute.

I remember bringing him home and he fell down the stairs and hit his head. Brittany thought he was going to die. He was a couple grand I think — a lot to spend on a dog.

The thing is, that dog attached himself to me. Not Brittany. It might have been a gift for her, but she could see it and I could see it — that dog loved me. He followed me everywhere. He slept with me at my feet. If I was in the house, he was there by my side. He was my best friend at the time.

---

## Missouri (Misery)

We left Japan. I left Japan kicking and screaming for a place called Missouri. But I called it Misery.

A different job — no longer AFN. Public affairs. I hated it. It was just logistics and red tape. A little bit of video work but it wasn't the same. The passion was gone for me. The leadership was different. Working at AFN, a lot of times felt like I was a civilian. This felt more like the military. It wasn't fun. It was work.

I was growing very far apart from Brittany. I got invested into VR — virtual reality. I bought an HTC Vive headset and I spent a lot of time in VR.

My kids always wanted to play it as well. But I never let them.

Saying that makes me cry now. I'm getting emotional just thinking about it. Jude and Bryden — I wish I had spent more time with them. I wish that I had made them more important in my life. I was such a fool.

I have to be gentle. I have to remember that I've been battling with mental health issues my whole life.

I hated Missouri. And so I put in for Korea. I wanted to get out of there as fast as I could. And I did. I was there less than a year before they picked me up for Korea, with a follow-on to Misawa. And I thought — okay, alright, I'm getting a second chance. I'll do this right this time.

I didn't know what I was supposed to do right. I was still an idiot.

---

## Korea — The Dark Year

With my marriage basically over without me even knowing it, I kept it going because Brittany had always liked Misawa too. But I couldn't take my family to Korea. And so I left. Truly left. I wasn't just no longer there in person — I had left a long time ago. I never existed really in my family. But I was gone both in mind and body this time.

I left for a year to Korea. Which was where I first started to really look at the demons. I didn't understand them, but I was seeing them.

Korea is the first place where I let it happen. Where I let myself attempt it. The thing that no one should ever do.

I think maybe instinctively I knew being alone in that room — which very much resembled a cell — was not going to be healthy. And it wasn't. I drank many days while in Korea. Alex didn't really help that because he's even self-admitted that he's an alcoholic. But that's what we did — we hung out, we drank. I played VR.

I got really into VR porn. It's sad thinking about — to be a disembodied being watching in that room. A young man alone, depressed, confused, in virtual reality. Not even in reality. That's how far into the computer screen I had gone.

I didn't even talk to my family much. Calls were infrequent. They were living their own life. I think Brittany probably had decided to divorce me sometime during the Korea thing. Maybe she was hoping that I would come back different.

---

## The Wretched and Friends in Korea

I started a project in Korea — The Wretched. Got Alex involved even though he had no experience. Told him it'd be easy. I lied. He started picking up 3D modeling. We started talking with Nautilus on the phone and he kind of got invested too — music and stuff, he said he would help with. We built it in Unity for a whole year. That's really what we worked on.

Work was still public affairs but it was a little different being overseas. There were some fun moments. We had a little trio — me, Alex, and Laurel. Laurel was from actually AFN Misawa — she was there with me and Scott. We weren't close or anything, but well enough that we had hung out a few times.

Alex had a crush on her. But we all hung out. Eventually the crush came out and she made it clear that she just wanted to be friends. And it was fine — Alex was able to do that.

That little trio kept us together.

---

## The Night in Korea

The night that I attempted it, they were both in Alex's room. And I just came in there and asked if I could stay there that night. We all slept in the same bed watching Rick and Morty.

If I hadn't had those people in my life in Korea, I probably wouldn't be here today.

---

## Pride — The Big Thread

Alex is still around. In fact, he's the only one of my friends that knows about what's going on in my life right now.

We played a lot of games too. I got them into Magic the Gathering and we played the Game of Thrones board game. Me and Alex only got into one real fight while in Korea, and it was over that board game. Because I hated losing and he beat me. And I don't even remember what set it off — my pride. It's always been my pride that set off my anger. Because I always feel like I had to be better than someone. Smarter. More capable.

That ties back to the stepfather thread. The big one. It's one of the big ones besides the porn addiction. It may even be bigger than the porn addiction.

---

## Misawa Denied — Minot Chosen

The military changed my orders in the last month while I was in Korea. They said it was because my wife had depression and that they couldn't treat her there. Which I tried to fight — she was diagnosed with depression while she was in Misawa. So I couldn't understand the logic.

I tried every loophole. Went to the legal department. Nothing. I couldn't do anything. You can't fight the government. The government says go, you go.

And so instead of Misawa, Japan — I came to Minot, North Dakota. Why not Minot.

---

## Arriving in Minot

I remember driving into Minot for the first time. Driving into North Dakota, taking note of how flat it was. Almost seemed like there was nothing here. I was in such a shock not being able to go back to Japan.

Close to the end of the trip, we started to run out of gas. There was no gas stations in sight. My oldest son Jude started to panic about the gas. I think it might have been the first time that I noticed how much anxiety he had. I didn't make it better — in fact, I kind of egged it on in some ways, if I remember correctly.

We ended up getting gas. Everything worked out. And we came into Minot.

---

## Marriage Ending in Minot

Stayed depressed. Made a couple friends. Tried to hang on to the marriage a little bit — not really for any reason other than sex, I guess.

Eventually Brittany came to me and said, "I want a divorce." And I was already ready to give it to her. I said, "Let's do it." I don't think she was expecting that. I think she was expecting me to fight.

We fought. She accused me of cheating on her when I was in Korea. Which I didn't. I've only ever had sex with two people in my whole life. I've only ever had sex with one person that I actually love.

---

## The Boy Who Cried Wolf

The night Brittany said she wanted a divorce — my first run-in with the law. Military police.

Brittany was going on about how terrible I had been. To be fair, most of her words were true. We had become roommates. I just used her for sex when I could, which was like never. I didn't treat her right. I didn't see all of this at that time — I just despised her.

We got in a fight and she said she was going to take the kids. I countered with, "If you take the kids, I'll kill you."

I didn't mean it. I've never meant anything like that. It was just something I said in passing. Which, to think about it now, she deserved to have the kids. She was the one that raised them. I was the absentee father.

I slept on the couch that night. And somewhere around midnight or so, I heard knocking at the door.

She had talked to her parents — which is strange because she didn't really like her parents. Very different views — she was very liberal, her parents were very conservative. She didn't talk to them, didn't like them. But I guess she had told them that night about what I had said. And they called the military police.

I sat in a holding cell for a night. First time I had ever experienced that. Taking me away in cuffs. Crying. I felt anger towards her.

It affected my military career a little bit. In the end it didn't do anything bad, but I did get paperwork. I had to get in my blues and stand in front of the commander and explain myself. It was a lot of drama. But it kind of let me completely let go of Brittany. Having to go through all that — it made it easier.

---

## The Car

Before we get to the best part, we need to talk about my car. The car I'm in right now — although 6 years ago it looked a little different. Wasn't missing so many pieces. Not so beat up. Kind of like me.

It was fresh. 2017. Was only a year old I believe. And the biggest purchase I've ever made in my life. I was like 34, 33, maybe 32. She's about to be paid off in about 2 or 3 months. I think I can do it in two.

When I first got her — leather seats. I took such good care of her. I had spent so much time in the military making money yet I never really saw any of it because it just kind of went to Brittany and she managed it.

This car was the first time I ever spent real money. Granted it was on a loan, of course. But it was my first big purchase of my life. Can you imagine that? 34-ish, whatever age I was. Most people have purchased houses and things.

I was so particular about it. I'd get angry when the kids would get their feet on the back of the car, make it dirty. Seems so stupid now, especially looking at the state it currently is in. An icon of Jesus covers a crack in the passenger window — more on that later. But that window hasn't broken. It's fractured. As shattered as it looks, it's been holding for some years now.

This car is the first place that I kissed the person that I want to kiss last. The person that I want to hold hands with on my deathbed. Be the last person I kiss. And I kissed her first in this car. Right next to me, where there's a bunch of trash — sauces from Domino's, empty Domino's bag, Taco bag.

I wish I could go back to that day and relive it.

---

## Finding Kyleen — Dating Apps

I signed up for every dating app that I could — Bumble, Tinder, all of them. And I put my profile on there. Was as honest as I could be. I don't know if I put that I was divorced — I think maybe I just put separated.

That was about the time that I went on a TDY and saw Nautilus for the first time in years. And it was a good day. I remember that day — felt like I was a teenager again. I talked to him about The Wretched and a new project that I was starting to spin up in my head about AI.

This was just the forefront of AI — before we had LLMs. And I was using the dating sites a lot and I kept thinking about things like a twin. Like a digital twin. I remember the idea I had was that you would have a digital twin that basically knew you inside and out and would go out to talk to other digital twins on the internet to find your perfect partner. Your perfect match. They would have their whole lives, your life, a mirror. And they'd be able to do simulated dating quickly to see who they would match with, who they would actually be good with. Basically a dating app with actually simulated dates. And that would match you.

I remember talking about it with Nautilus.

I don't think I had matched with Kyleen yet at that point. I had matched with a couple other people that I had sent messages to but no replies or anything like that. And it might have been very soon after I came back from that short TDY that I got a match with Ky. It might have been during that. I want to say it was right after.

---

## The First Message

She was pretty. Her pictures — they were pretty. And I noticed that she had had a kid. And so I thought that was a good thing because she could understand.

The first message I sent to her, I believe, was somewhere along the lines of: "I like your hair."

What a line.

---

## First Date — Coffee

We texted for a few days. Eventually got her phone number, maybe day two or three. Started talking. I was still living in the house on base. Brittany was still living there too. I was sleeping on the couch, although I was looking at buying my own bed. And I did get it, I think, not too shortly after that. Set up the bed in the living room, which is where I had my computer and the VR.

Me and Ky started talking. And talking on the phone. I remember talking about Caesar's — which is a wild thing to talk about. I don't remember if that conversation happened before or after we met, but I know the conversations were intense just from the beginning. At least for me.

She was quiet. Not as quiet as she was in person when I finally did meet her, but reserved.

I asked for a coffee date. I think she might have even turned me down the first time. But the state fair was in town and she was going to see Weezer. Which was funny because I was using songs and some of my products at work from Weezer — a big one actually. I think we even got Weezer to comment on it if I remember correctly.

She finally convinced — I got her to go get a coffee with me. It was right after the concert and she had drank a whole lot that night and she was hungover. She tried to get out of it — told me she didn't even want to go.

We met in that parking lot for the first time. My shiny new car. Hers, an older hybrid. I took note of that.

We went into the coffee shop. The first thing I noticed is she didn't look quite like her pictures. But who does? She was kind of frumpy looking honestly. She hadn't dressed up or anything. I think she was wearing some shorts and a t-shirt. And she looked a little hungover for sure.

In some ways I didn't even really see her as that attractive when I first met her.

---

## The Walk — Downtown Minot

I could tell she was nervous. I did all the talking. I remember going to get a coffee and I could just tell she was nervous and she was being hesitant. I was like, "Are you going to get a coffee, or what are we doing?" She didn't seem to want to — I later found out it was because she had no money to buy a coffee. Which of course I bought for her.

We got coffees and decided to walk. We walked around downtown Minot. It was kind of the first time I'd ever really done that, even though I had been here for a while. Just talked. You know, the stuff that people talk about on the first date — who are you, what do you like.

But really, it all came from me. I just pulled — like I'm doing now. And I could see something in her. I don't know what it was, but I couldn't quite peg it. And that was weird for me — someone who had interviewed hundreds of people, who played games as a kid imagining that he was a different person. I've often seen myself as an empath. But I couldn't build her. I knew she was shy, but it was like I couldn't quite peg it. Wasn't just shyness. Maybe it was like a feeling that there was something there — like a loneliness or a pain that I kind of attached to. Maybe even thought we were similar in some way.

I ran through all kinds of things — movies, books, talked about Star Wars. She said her dad was into Star Wars but she never really watched them (that's changed now — she got into Star Wars).

She shared some details. Surface-level things. We walked around, sat on a park bench. I ended up leaving my phone there — we had to walk and find it. Figure out where I left it. It was embarrassing. But it was like a warning for her — hey, this is who I am. I forget things. I leave things places. ADHD is real.

---

## Scandinavian Park

I didn't want it to end. I didn't know why. Here is this frumpy girl — I mean, I could see it, you know. If she had made herself up, she was beautiful. But it wasn't like an immediate physical attraction to her. It was like — I wanted to know. I wanted to know who this person was. And I couldn't figure it out.

And so I extended. I said, "Let's go somewhere else." I didn't really know where to go. I think it was her that mentioned the Scandinavian Park. So we went there, drove there. Got out and walked around the Scandinavian Park and talked some more.

I elevated the questions a bit. Got into more serious things — things that you probably shouldn't talk about on a first date. Politics, life views. I made it apparent that I was pretty liberal. She commented something about her brother being gay, which made me relate to my friend Scott.

We talked about other things like religion — which I hadn't figured out at that point. At the time I was pretty convinced that things were a simulation, a computer simulation of some sort. I might have even mentioned those things to her. I wonder what she was thinking at that time. I've asked about it, and from what it seems like, it was just a physical attraction for her in the beginning.

---

## The First Kiss

We sat in the park and it started to rain a little bit. So we went back to the car. A little bit of fog in the car. And I looked over and I still didn't want to just let go. I felt like if this was the end of the day, then I wouldn't get a chance to figure out who this girl was. This enigma.

She sat there. Awkwardly. Me sitting there. I asked an awkward question: "Can I kiss you?"

I think it threw her off. She told me later that no one had ever asked her to kiss her before.

She said yes.

I kissed her. And that was the start of everything.

---

## Getting Physical — Complications

It got physical fast. That was what I was looking for, right? And I guess that's what she was looking for too — Tinder's mostly for hookups. I didn't really know that.

But there were complications in the beginning. I was still living with my wife. The Air Force — that is a big no-no. Even if you are going through a divorce, you can't do that. You can get kicked out for that kind of thing.

And Kyleen — she was still living with her ex that she had had a child with. They hadn't married, but it was an interesting time.

I think it added a little bit of tension. Which, all my studying — that's kind of a motivator for a dismissive avoidant. That sense of "you can't really have me." Where is this going?

Our first sexual encounter was in this car. Although I was unable to perform. And that was a lot. The beginning of our relationship was intense, but I had trouble physically. I was on meds and I attributed it to that, and stress, and exiting and entering a relationship at the same time, and a lot of things. Depression. Finally kind of coming full circle to my life and looking at it. Feeling ashamed about my kids. Wondering what the future held. It was a lot.

She was understanding. Kind about it. Even with the Valentine's Day game that we played recently, some of those questions came up and I got the full picture. For her, it was fun, exciting. And I think because of the problems that I had, she saw it as a challenge. And knowing her personality now, she felt like it was a little bit her fault, and so that discouraged her but also encouraged her. In a way it was a good thing — more of that uncertainty, can't quite have what you want.

---

## The Early Days

They were exciting. Brittany found out because I couldn't keep my mouth shut — I told friends at work because I was excited. I wanted to share it with the world even though I couldn't. "Look at this beautiful woman that I met — she's interested in me."

I ended up taking her to the Air Force ball. Never took Brittany to the Air Force ball once — and she made that clear.

Brittany threatened to tell my supervisors about it because she also knew about the UCMJ stuff. Last big fight. I remember her getting so irate that I recorded it on my phone. I still have that video of Brittany yelling at me. I watched it recently and I could see what was really happening — she was hurt. She had spent 10 years of her life and I was just throwing it away as fast as I could. Moving on. I feel bad for that. But it was for the best for both of us.

I got in my car that night. I went to Ky. We drove around. I think I remember watching the movie Scott Pilgrim on our phone. Trying to have sex in the backseat — not having too much luck with it. Movies romanticize cars and sex, but it's a difficult thing to do, especially if you're already having other issues.

A lot of the first moments were really established in that car. Driving around, even going to a spooky old abandoned house, talking to each other, relating our situations. She was aware of what could happen if the Air Force found out. I think that added some intensity to it too.

---

## Moving In Together

I remember sneaking into her house — our house, although she calls it her house now. But I still see it as our house. I put 6 years into that house. Maybe I didn't do the best job that it could have been done, but I did my damnedest. Taking care of, cleaning it, managing it.

I snuck in the basement window because that's where she was staying. Her ex-boyfriend had taken the bedroom — which just seemed crazy to me. Even if you're in a separation, just seems right to give the woman the bedroom. That's what I was doing for Brittany, and I didn't even really like Brittany.
